issue_title: Texas Supreme Court opinions missing
issue_body:
The Texas Supreme Court's clerk tweeted that he thought it'd be cool if SCOTUS online dockets linked back to lower court online dockets. He included a URL to one SCOTUS docket, which had a docket number for a Texas Supreme Court case, _Pidgeon_ (15-0688).

* Tweet: https://twitter.com/blakeahawthorne/status/930817357030752257
* Supreme Court docket: https://www.supremecourt.gov/search.aspx?filename=/docket/docketfiles/html/public/17-424.html
* SCOTX opinion: http://www.txcourts.gov/media/1438061/150688.pdf

I wanted to see if we could match the SCOTUS docket back to the SCOTX opinion on CourtListener using just the court and docket number. However, CourtListener does not have this SCOTX opinion! It was filed June 30, 2017. In fact, we don't have seem to have any SCOTX opinions from June 30 to July 5, 2017: https://www.courtlistener.com/?q=&type=o&order_by=dateFiled+desc&filed_after=2017-06-10&filed_before=2017-07-05&court=tex. However, the _Pidgeon_ case does appear on SCOTX's weekly opinion page for June 30, 2017 (http://www.txcourts.gov/supreme/orders-opinions/2017/june/june-30-2017/). 

We seem to be missing other recent SCOTX opinions as well.

* SCOTX's November 10, 2017 weekly opinion page: http://www.txcourts.gov/supreme/orders-opinions/2017/november/november-10-2017/
* CourtListener results for SCOTX opinions between Nov 9 and Nov 11 **(none)**: https://www.courtlistener.com/?q=&type=o&order_by=dateFiled+desc&filed_after=2017-11-09&filed_before=2017-11-11&court=tex

I looked briefly at the Texas courts scraper, and it seems to be [using a search form](https://github.com/freelawproject/juriscraper/blob/master/juriscraper/opinions/united_states/state/tex.py#L50). However, there are also a weekly opinion pages, available here: http://www.txcourts.gov/supreme/orders-opinions/.  I confirmed that you can indeed find the _Pidgeon_ opinion through the form.

I did not check to see if other Texas courts' opinions are also missing.

issue_title: Attach volume to app
issue_body:
**Problem**
I am trying to host a Prestashop. I'll try to refrain myself from judging and will stick to objective facts, but Prestashop is a pile of crap. Basically, it does not allow for any kind of abstraction and does really stupid things with image files (idiotic rewriting rules, non-configurable storage directory, ...).

In order to adapt this to the 12-factor concepts, I need to store those images in an external service, like S3. This way, images are automatically available from everywhere. Especially with something like yas3fs which handles caching files and mounting the same bucket from several locations.

**Solution**
What would be interesting would be to be able to attach another app (or something similar) as Docker Volume Plugins that will be started alongside the main app, with the same config (so you can get credentials from environment).

Something like

```
cd plugin-app
deis create --storage-plugin plugin-app
git push origin master

cd ../main-app
deis create main-app volume_name:/app/img plugin-app
deis config:set TOKEN=xxxxxx
git push origin master
```

issue_title: Add a method to enable storops logging to console
issue_body: Storops doesn't configure the logging by default. The user need to configure the logging by themselves in order to see the log messages. It is not convenient when we try storops in console such as python or ipython.  A simple API to enable the console logging will be very useful when we try the storops in console for testing or POC purpose.

issue_title: Bug Using `impute` With The Robot Failure Dataset
Inconsistent encoding of 0 in int_to_big_endian
issue_body:
Python 3 and python 2.7 implementations encode 0 in diffent ways:

```
Python 3.5.2 (default, Jul  5 2016, 12:43:10) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import rlp
>>> rlp.utils.int_to_big_endian(0)
b''
>>> 
>python2
Python 2.7.12 (default, Jul  1 2016, 15:12:24) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import rlp
>>> rlp.utils.int_to_big_endian(0)
'\x00'
```

I think, the python3 encoding is the right one.  0 should be encoded by a zero length bytestring.

issue_body:
* Version: 4.3.0
* Python: 3.6
* OS: osx


### What was wrong?

I have a contract that emits event that I want to filter. When setting the `topics` explicitly I get less events than without specifying it, event though `[]`should mean everything according to the RPC specs: https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/JSON-RPC#eth_newfilter

```py
    f1 = web3.eth.filter({
        'fromBlock': 0,
        'address': token_network_address,
    })

    f2 = web3.eth.filter({

        'address': token_network_address,

    e1 = f1.get_all_entries()
    e2 = f2.get_all_entries()

    print(len(e1))
    print(len(e2))
```

returns in my case, which is a eth-tester chain. Also `e1` includes every event twice.

56
28
```


### How can it be fixed?

Not sure if my expectation is wrong, but I expect both calls to return the same objects.

issue_title: itsdb.TestSuite.write() documentation doesn't match implementation
issue_body:
The documentation for `itsdb.TestSuite.write()` says its first argument is "an iterable of names of tables to write", but it in fact expects a mapping of table names to iterables of Records, or to `None`, which uses the stored records for the table.

It would be convenient and not terribly hard to accept several argument types:

```python
ts = TestSuite(...)
# all tables
ts.write()
# just the item table
ts.write('item')
# the item and parse tables
ts.write(['item', 'parse'])
# write item_rows to item, and the stored parse records to parse
ts.write({'item': item_rows, 'parse': None})
```

The docstring also needs to be updated to be correct WRT the implementation.

issue_title: Allow and/or document preprocessor usage with the ACE interface
issue_body:
gTest allows preprocessors in the ART command like this:

```python
ace_cmd = '{prep}ace -g {gram} {opts}'.format(
            prep=ace_preprocessor + ' | ' if ace_preprocessor else '',
            gram=grm,
            opts=' '.join(ace_options or [])
        )
        subprocess.check_call(
            ['art', '-a', ace_cmd, dest_dir] + (options or []),
            stdout=log, stderr=log, close_fds=True
        )
```

But it's not possible, or at least not clear how, to use a preprocessor in pyDelphin with the ACE interface directly.

issue_title: itsdb unescaping has a problem

Long derivation strings returned by the itsdb module from parsed profiles only unescape things like quotes part-way through the string. See this example from the jh2 corpus:

```

] ]")) (421 sp-hd_n_c -0.296298 1 5 (144 the_1 -1.10722 1 2 ("the" 55 "token [ +CARG #1=\"the\" +CLASS alphabetic [ +CASE non_capitalized+lower +INITIAL - ] +FORM #1 +FROM #2=\"3\" +ID *diff-list* [ LAST #3=*list* LIST *cons* [ FIRST \"2\" REST #3 ] ] +PRED predsort +TICK bool +TNT null_tnt [ +MAIN tnt_main [ +PRB \"1\" +TAG \"DT\" ] +PRBS *null* +TAGS *null* ] +TO \"6\" +TRAIT token_trait [ +HD token_head [ +LL ctype [ -CTYPE- string ] +TG string +TI #2 ] +IT italics +LB bracket_null +RB bracket_null +UW - ] ]")) (420 hdn-aj_redrel_c 0.589321 2 5 (416 n_ms-cnt_ilr 0.122326 2 3 (170 spirit_n1 -0.149109 2 3 ("spirit" 57 "token [ +CARG #1=\"spirit\" +CLASS alphabetic [ +CASE non_capitalized+lower +INITIAL - ] +FORM #1 +FROM #2=\"7\" +ID *diff-list* [ LAST #3=*list* LIST *cons* [ FIRST \"3\" REST #3 ] ] +PRED predsort +TICK bool +TNT null_tnt [ +MAIN tnt_main [ +PRB \\"1\\" +TAG \\"NN\\" ] +PRBS *null* +TAGS *null* ] +TO \\"13\\" +TRAIT token_trait [ +HD token_head [ +LL ctype [ -CTYPE- string ] +TG string +TI #2 ] +IT italics +LB bracket_null +RB bracket_null +UW - ] ]"))) (419 hd-cmp_u_c -0.618316 3 5 (204 of_poss -1.03414 3 4 ("of" 59 "token [ +CARG #1=\\"of\\" +CLASS alphabetic [ +CASE non_capitalized+lower +INITIAL - ] +FORM #1 +FROM #2=\\"14\\" +ID *diff-list* [ LAST #3=*list* LIST *cons* [ FIRST \\"4\\" REST #3 ] ] +PRED predsort +TICK bool +TNT null_tnt [ +MAIN tnt_main [ +PRB \\"1\\" +TAG \\"IN\\" ] +PRBS *null* +TAGS *null* ] +TO \\"16\\" +TRAIT token_trait [ +HD token_head [ +LL ctype [ -CTYPE- string ] +TG string +TI #2 ] +IT italics +LB bracket_null +RB bracket_null +UW - ] ]")) (418 hdn_bnp-pn_c -0.436173 4 5 (417 n_sg_ilr -0.444903 4 5 (288 vinje_n1 -0.010981 4 5 ("vinje" 65 "token [ +CARG \\"Vinje\\" +CLASS alphabetic [ +CASE capitalized+lower +INITIAL - ] +FORM \\"vinje\\" +FROM #1=\\"17\\" +ID *diff-list* [ LAST #2=*list* LIST *cons* [ FIRST \\"5\\" REST #2 ] ] +PRED predsort +TICK bool +TNT null_tnt [ +MAIN tnt_main [ +PRB \\"0.91270569999999995\\" +TAG \\"NNP\\" ] +PRBS *null* +TAGS *null* ] +TO \\"22\\" +TRAIT token_trait [ +HD token_head [ +LL ctype [ -CTYPE- string ] +TG string +TI #1 ] +IT italics +LB bracket_null +RB bracket_null +UW - ] ]"))))))))))
```

Note that `FIRST \"3\"` is the last instance with properly unescaped quotes. The following `+PRB \\"1\\"` is still escaped.

This is because of a malformed call to `re.sub()`:

```python
    return re.sub(r'(\\s|\\n|\\\\)', _unescape, string, re.UNICODE)
```

The 4th argument of `re.sub()` is not the flags, but the count, and `re.UNICODE` has a value of 32. This can be fixed by using a keyword argument:

```python
    return re.sub(r'(\\s|\\n|\\\\)', _unescape, string, flags=re.UNICODE)
```

Hi!  Read this; it's important.

This is an issue tracker for conda -- the package manager.  File feature requests
for conda here, as well as bug reports about something conda has messed up.

If your issue is a bug report or feature request for:

  * all other conda commands that start with 'conda':
      ==> please file it here!

If you continue on, and especially if you are submitting a bug report,

   **please include all requested information below.**

If a maintainer determines the information is required to understand 
your issue, and if it is not provided, your issue may be
closed automatically.

-->

**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

If linting the codebase with `pycodestyle`, the replacement for `pep8`, it emits a deprecation warning:
```
pycodestyle.py:2423: UserWarning: [pep8] section is deprecated. Use [pycodestyle].

### Steps to Reproduce
<!-- If the current behavior is a bug, please provide specific, minimal steps to independently reproduce.
     Include the exact conda commands that reproduce the issue and their output between the ticks below. -->

`conda install pycodestyle`, then run it against any file in the repository.




## Expected Behavior
<!-- What do you think should happen? -->

Remove deprecation warning by updating section block in setup.cfg.

issue_title: conda command not found after running conda update package

Using conda 3.10.1-py34_0 on OS10.10.3, I wanted to update one of our in-house packages, and so created the package appropriately, and ran:
$ conda update my-package

In this update, it found the new version of conda (3.13.0-py34_0), downloaded that and continued the update. The update failed in the package linking step, citing an "unexpected error". Further explorations after the crash revealed that the command 'conda' was no longer in anaconda/bin/

issue_title: conda search --platform does not search other platforms
issue_body:
The `--platform` argument of the `conda search` subcommand does not search for packages on other platforms.  

For example:

```
~$ conda info
Current conda install:

               platform : linux-64
          conda version : 4.2.7
       conda is private : False
      conda-env version : 4.2.7
    conda-build version : 1.21.14+0.g4dfebe9.dirty
         python version : 3.5.2.final.0
       requests version : 2.10.0
       root environment : /home/jhelmus/anaconda  (writable)
    default environment : /home/jhelmus/anaconda
       envs directories : /home/jhelmus/anaconda/envs
          package cache : /home/jhelmus/anaconda/pkgs
           channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64/
                          https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch/
                          https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/linux-64/
                          https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/noarch/
            config file : /home/jhelmus/.condarc
           offline mode : False
~$ conda --version
conda 4.2.7
~$ conda search --platform win-64 gawk
```

Note that there is a gawk-3.1.6-0.tar.bz2 package in the [default win-64 repo](https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/).  

This may be because the `get_index` function in the conda.api module takes a **platform** argument but this argument is not used in the function.

Thanks for the report.  As far as I've been able to tell, these errors were always here, but no logger handler was set up, so these messages were not being shown.  Conda-build recently added logger handlers, and sets these to show warnings and above from conda.  Removing the trash is part of conda, though.  I'm moving this issue to the conda issue tracker.

issue_title: `conda activate` on 4.4.7 doesn't complete unless I press enter twice
issue_body:
I'm experiencing a strange issue. I was running conda `4.4.3`, and was experiencing issues with extreme slowness to activate the base environment. The time to run `conda activate` was really long, we're talking several minutes. I've had this issue ever since I upgraded to conda `4.4.3` from `4.3.<something>`. 
I updated to version `4.4.7` just now and after some trial and error I realized that running `conda activate` doesn't complete until I press Enter a few times.
First I thought it was a random fluke, but some more testing showed that it activates without problems in under a second if I just press Enter twice after calling `conda activate`. 

My conda installation is fairly old and has been in use since about a year ago (original install date was beginning of February 2017, I think). I'm running Scientific Linux 7.4 (i.e. CentOS).

What can I do to troubleshoot this, to find what's causing this issue?

issue_title: Allow management of activate.d environment variables in environment.yml files
issue_body:
This is a new feature proposal, to extend the format for the yml files usable to create conda environments, to allow expression of environment variables (with values) to be statically set. I propose:

Another toplevel in that file titled environment, with k=v elements under it being parsed as environment variables to set in the environment. Example:

my_tensorflow_environment.yml
channels:
- defaults
dependencies:
- keras
- tensorflow
- python=3.6
environment:
- KERAS_BACKEND=tensorflow
- AWS_KEY=a32131231231
- AWS_SECRET=a123123123123

When parsing this via "conda install -c my_tensorflow_environment.yml -n tensor", conda would populate $INSTALL/envs/tensor/etc/conda/activate.d/env_vars.sh and deactivate.d/env_vars.sh accordingly.

The purposes of this proposal are:
1) For people making software that's meant to run in a conda environment, to let the environment.yml fully express what's needed for the environment to be set up correctly so we can keep these instructions out of README files, and
2) To bridge a last bit of gap between containers and conda

If people like this proposal I'd be happy to work on it.

issue_title: `defaults` in enterprise context interpreted as public repo, not local private repo
issue_body:
[environment.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/1665627/environment.txt)
This is with `conda` v 4.3.14 in an Anaconda Enterprise deployment context (AE4.1.2).  Using an `environment.yml` file the `defaults` channel alias is interpreted as `repo.continuum.io` which is (unsurprisingly) not accessible from the firewalled corporate network where AE4 is installed.  It seems like the configuration, which causes all other `conda` operations to look at the local Anaconda repository server, should be picked up in a way that the `defaults` alias refers to the associated/equivalent channels on the local system.

```

issue_title: Installing conda-build==1.19 while conda pinned deletes $/HOME/miniconda/bin/activate
issue_body:
Due to the fix for issue https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2524 not yet being released (https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh points to 4.0.5 not 4.1.x) I am pinning conda to 3.19.3.  This worked up until recently (I just noticed the problem today).  The code below demonstrates how installing conda-build can delete $HOME/miniconda/bin/activate thus making it impossible to activate any environment.

```
#!/bin/bash
set -e

rm -rf $HOME/miniconda
rm -rf Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh

wget https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
chmod +x ./Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh

./Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -b -p $HOME/miniconda
$HOME/miniconda/bin/conda install -y conda=3.19.3
echo "conda ==3.19.3" >> $HOME/miniconda/conda-meta/pinned

find $HOME/miniconda -name activate
conda install conda-build==1.19 -y
find $HOME/miniconda -name activate
```

The first find call outputs this:

```
$HOME/miniconda/pkgs/conda-env-2.4.5-py27_0/bin/activate
$HOME/miniconda/bin/activate
```

The second find call outputs this:

```
$HOME/miniconda/pkgs/conda-env-2.4.5-py27_0/bin/activate
```

issue_title: Concurrent writes to the same package cache
issue_body:
This issue concerns this bit from the release notes for 4.3.0:

> Locking has never been fully effective in conda, and it often created a false sense of security. In this release, multi-user package cache support has been implemented for improved safety by hard-linking packages in read-only caches to the user's primary user package cache. Still, users are cautioned that undefined behavior can result when conda is running in multiple process and operating on the same package caches and/or environments.

Now, I understand why concurrent writes to the same environment are essentially impossible to support. There's just too many ways in which an environment can be mutated. But package caches are another story. My understanding is that the only way they are mutated is that tarballs get downloaded/copied into them, and then those tarballs get unpacked. (And there's also conda clean, I guess). And we should be able to assume that if two processes are trying to download/unpack a particular package at the same time, neither of them care which one of the two "succeeds". So it seems like it should be quite feasible to use atomic filesystem operations to fully support concurrent writes to the same package cache as a guaranteed feature. The basic approach would be to unpack the tarball under a temporary name, then do an os.rename to the final name, which is guaranteed to fail if the destination already exists. So the first process to create the directory would "win", which is exactly what we want because it guarantees that no one creates hardlinks to a package which is then overwritten by another process unpacking the same package.

Is conda already doing anything like this? If not, are there any major difficulties with this sort of thing that I'm missing?

For context, my team is considering a multi-user setup where users all have their own environments (and perhaps where temporary environments are routinely created and torn down on the fly), but we are hoping to keep disk usage under control by having a global package cache that is writable to everyone. But if a shared writable package cache is undefined behavior, then clearly this sort of setup would not work.

issue_title: Add "Portable" command line option to miniconda installer
issue_body:
We've tried to deploy a web application on Azure's web apps, using the silent install functionality of the installer. Since we're deploying to a sandbox there, we can't modify global machine state, such as the registry. We thought that we could install portably, but of course, among others, the installer registers the uninstaller, and this causes the deployment to fail. See https://github.com/projectkudu/kudu/issues/1862

I've tried to find the source for your .msi installer so I could open a PR, but I guess they're closed source as I couldn't find them. Can you add an option to the installer so miniconda installs 100% portable, e.g. without touching the registry, program files and environment variables?

issue_title: vsyscall call breaks conda on modern debian and under lxss (Linux on Windows)
issue_body:
The conda gcc package (and probably multiple other packages) segfault when used on newer Debian and when running under Linux on Windows emulation.

This is because the conda gcc package is linked against a very old version of glibc (older than 2.14 which was released in 2011!). This old version of glibc uses the ["vsyscall" functionality](https://lwn.net/Articles/446528/) which is not available on new Linux distributions and on Linux on Windows. 

vsyscall was deprecated in Linux in 2011 (!!). Debian has now disable emulation in the kernel and this causes all apps to crash when started. It is also considered insecure to have it enabled.

Newer glibc versions (2.14) have removed the usage of this functionality.

issue_title: conda create does not lock anything
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [x] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior

Conda apparently does not make use of locks when modifying environments or package cache.


### Steps to Reproduce

```
conda create -n env1 --file env.txt &
conda create -n env2 --file env.txt &
conda create -n env3 --file env.txt
```


## Expected Behavior

Parallel invokation of `conda` commands should be handled in some sensible fashion. The simplest may be outright denial to have more than one `conda` instance operating on the same conda installation. Neat would of course be fine-grained locking of indices, package-cache entries and environments with conda instances neatly waiting on one another.

Curiously, there is a module `conda.lock`, but it does not appear to be used by e.g. `conda create`? Was locking perhaps previously in place but lost at some point?

issue_title: After activating an environment `libselinux.so.1: cannot open shared object file`
issue_body:
I have an `environment.yml` file as pasted bellow. When I create an environment from it, and activate it, I see some weird bugs:

```bash
(myenv) $ vim
vim: error while loading shared libraries: libselinux.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

(myenv) $ /bin/python
Could not find platform independent libraries <prefix>
Could not find platform dependent libraries <exec_prefix>
Consider setting $PYTHONHOME to <prefix>[:<exec_prefix>]
Fatal Python error: Py_Initialize: Unable to get the locale encoding
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'encodings'

Current thread 0x00007f30a8f08700 (most recent call first):
[1]    7891 abort (core dumped)  /bin/python
```

I have no idea if they are related, but it never happens when I'm not in this conda environment, so I guess they are. Notice also that I'm not using any alternative channels.

Good chancces that this is a bug in a specific package I use, and will be happy to get some hints for how to check it. However, I believe that it also related to conda, because I can create an empty environment, activate it, update with the environment file, and the bugs disappear:

```bash
$ conda create -n myenv python=3.6
...
$ source activate myenv
(myenv) $ vim  # works fine
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # works fine
(myenv) $ conda env update -f environment.yml
(myenv) $ vim  # still works fine
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # still works fine
(myenv) $ source deactivate
$ source activate myenv
(myenv) $ vim  # broken again
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # broken again
```

## Software

conda: 4.3.16
os: Arch linux 4.11.7-1
gvim: 8.0
system python: 3.6.1

## Notes

- I failed to reproduce it on another machine (conda 4.3.21, CentOS, vim 7.4.160, system python 2.7.5).
- Seems like the same issue as [here](https://stackoverflow.com/q/40110207/1224456), but due to the above I really don't think that it should be solved by system wide changes.

issue_title: Investigate PID register in MX28 firmware
issue_body:
The "new" MX28 firmware seems to have  change the order of the [PID register to a DIP](http://support.robotis.com/en/product/dynamixel/mx_series/mx-28.htm).

 " __The control table’s order for PID has changed to DIP from this version onwards. Please make reference of this change.__"
But... it is not sure of which version changed this feature.

issue_title: Write the return_delay_time=0 by default
issue_body:
A recurent problem is that people forget to set the return_delay_time to 0ms (250ms by default), this leads to lot of timeout error and jerky mouvement.

During the robot instanciation we should verify this parameter and change it if needed.

```python

issue_title: pysam write appears to truncate files
issue_body:
Hi there,

I would like to split a very large bam file into subfiles, one for each chromosome and each with an appropriate header. Using pysam I have come up with the following:

```
#! /usr/bin/python

import pysam

PySam_test = pysam.AlignmentFile('test.bam')

chromosomes = zip(PySam_test.references, PySam_test.lengths)

def split_PySam_test_by_chromosome(chromosome):
    outfile = pysam.AlignmentFile("test_%s.bam" % chromosome[0], "wb", reference_names=[chromosome[0]], reference_lengths=[chromosome[1]])
    for alignment in PySam_test.fetch(reference=chromosome[0]): outfile.write(alignment)
    outfile.close()

map(split_PySam_test_by_chromosome, chromosomes)
```

Unfortunately, this seems to work for the first subfile only (i.e. test_chr1.bam). All the remaining files are written to disk and appear to have roughly the appropriate size, however, samtools view -h prints the header and then gives a truncated file error, e.g.: 

```
@SQ     SN:2    LN:242193529 
[main_samview] truncated file.
```

Looks as though the end of file is not indicated properly, but why does it work for the first file but not all the others when all of them are generated from the same loop?

Any help would be much appreciated.

Cheers,

mce

PS: In the end, I would like to make that run in paralell, i.e.

```
import multiprocessing
cpus = multiprocessing.cpu_count()

pool = multiprocessing.Pool(cpus)
pool.map(split_PySam_by_chromosome, chromosomes)
pool.close()
```
Hence the map statement.

issue_title: az login --service-principal --terraform
issue_body:
# Description

It would be nice to have an additional --terraform switch for  **az login** to pull in Terraform AzureRM provider service principal credentials, as per https://www.terraform.io/docs/providers/azurerm/authenticating_via_service_principal.html. 

These would either be exported environment variables, or a provider stanza from a *.tf file in the current directory.  

## Environment variables

Empty provider stanza in *.tf file:
```ruby
provider "azurerm" {}
```

Example environment variables:
```bash
export ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID="2d31be49-d959-4415-bb65-8aec2c90ba62"
export ARM_CLIENT_ID="c8adcaf8-9277-4307-965b-5904ba4afa8d"
export ARM_CLIENT_SECRET="0aad251a-f964-4628-a549-e1800d848146"
export ARM_TENANT_ID="72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47"
```

## Populated provider stanza

Example populated provider stanza in one of the *.tf file:
```ruby
provider "azurerm" {
  subscription_id = "2d31be49-d959-4415-bb65-8aec2c90ba62"
  client_id       = "c8adcaf8-9277-4307-965b-5904ba4afa8d"
  client_secret   = "4b372434-29eb-428d-8c90-fd67991dd086"
  tenant_id       = "72f988bf-86f1-41af-91ab-2d7cd011db47"
}
```

issue_title: Fileshare support
issue_body:
Hi,

I was wondering when will be available the support of File Share service in Storage accounts via the Azure Python SDK.

The File Share service is available in the .NET SDK:
http://azure.microsoft.com/en-in/documentation/articles/storage-dotnet-how-to-use-files/

In the meantime, I created a fork of the Azure SDK for Python that support the creation and deletion of file shares in Storage accounts. You can find the fork at:
https://github.com/dominoFire/azure-sdk-for-python

An example for creating a File Share is showed below:

```python
from azure.servicemanagement import *
from azure.storage.fileshareservice import FileShareService

# simple predicates over request status 
# You can find the complete test at
# https://bitbucket.org/dominofire/sweeper
from sweeper.cloud.azure.manager import wait_for_storage_account, wait_for_request_succeeded

#Azure subscription ID
subscription_id = '33232-...'
# path to .pem file that includes the certificate to the Azure Account
pem_file = 'cert.pem'
 
sms = ServiceManagementService(subscription_id, pem_file)

# For viewing the HTTP requests
# azure.http.httpclient.DEBUG_REQUESTS = True
# azure.http.httpclient.DEBUG_RESPONSES = True

res_name = 'creditgeometry'
file_share_name = 'myshare'
storage_account_result = sms.create_storage_account(service_name=res_name,
                                                    description='Storage account for Test {}'.format(res_name),
                                                    label=res_name,
                                                    geo_replication_enabled=None,
                                                    account_type='Standard_LRS',
                                                    location='West US')
wait_for_request_succeeded(storage_account_result.request_id)
wait_for_storage_account(res_name)
storage_keys = sms.get_storage_account_keys(service_name=res_name)
file_svc = FileShareService(account_name=res_name, account_key=storage_keys.storage_service_keys.primary)
file_svc.create_file_share(file_share_name, fail_on_exist=True)
file_svc.delete_file_share(file_share_name, fail_not_exist=True)
sms.delete_storage_account(res_name)
```

issue_title: Allow #include <bullet/LinearMath/...>
issue_body:
Currently the bullet header files are installed to `${CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX}/include/bullet`, which is good, but the exported include path points to this exact folder, so that users wishing to include bullet files must use the following:
~~~
#include <LinearMath/btVector3.h>
~~~

It would be nice to allow the following:
~~~
#include <bullet/LinearMath/btVector3.h>
~~~

This helps indicate where header files are coming from (since LinearMath is a pretty generic name) and helps avoid header file name conflicts (however unlikely they may be).

This could be fixed by adding an additional `-I` flag to the pkg-config CFLAGS (keeping the existing one for backwards compatibility).

  /usr/lib/python3.6/asyncio/unix_events.py:815: RuntimeWarning: !!! asyncio is trying to install its ChildWatcher for SIGCHLD signal !!!
  
  This is probably because a uvloop instance is used with asyncio.set_event_loop(). The correct way to use uvloop is to install its policy: `asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())`

issue_title: Make grading plots less horrible
issue_body:
Grading plots is a huge pain. Partially, they just need to be done manually, but there are also some things that could be autograded. However, if the plots are done with matplotlib at least, this is difficult, as the underlying data model doesn't necessarily correspond one-to-one with what the plot looks like. For example:

1. `plot` and `scatter` can produce nearly identical plots, but one is accessed through lines and one is accessed through collections.
2. It's possible to use `scatter` within a for loop for just a single point but with an array of colors, meaning that when we check the point colors they seem correct, but they're actually not because the points all just use the first color of the color array.
3. How to check things like a dendrogram plot?

This might not actually be something nbgrader wants to handle, especially if it wants to be language agnostic, but I'd really like a better way to do this so I'm opening an issue here for now anyway.

issue_title: Add an option to make grader blind to which student is being graded
issue_body: This can help eliminate well-known gender and race biases.

issue_title: Formgrader Jupyterhub Process Giving 404s for CSS/JS
issue_body: Just wanted to put this into the issues in case it comes up for others. There is an issue with the new method of using fromgrader on a JupyterHub instance as a managed jupyter notebook server. This seems to be a JupyterHub issue, not an nbgrader issue, but I wanted to make other users aware. I've described it [here (issue #997)](https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/issues/997) along with a workaround. If you start the managed JupyterHub process and are unable to view it, check your web console and see if you get `custom.js` and `custom.css` 404s. If so, see the the referenced issue.

issue_title: Jupyter 4.1.0rc1 %matplotlib notebook bug
issue_body:
Hi Jupyter devs,

In previous version of the notebook, executing a cell with `shift+enter` that contained matplotlib code to generate an interactive figure, say `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`, would cause the matplotlib figure to gain control over keyboard inputs and the notebook cell highlighting would remain on that same cell.  Pressing `shift+enter` again would return the keyboard control back to the notebook and move to the next notebook cell.

In `4.1.0rc1`, this behavior has changed and is almost certainly a bug.  [Here is an example notebook] (https://gist.github.com/ericdill/537effed420f22af82e0) to demonstrate this bug.  If you download this notebook and execute its cells with shift+enter, here is what seems to happen:

First `shift+enter`: executes the import cell.
Second `shift+enter`: creates the interactive matplotlib figure, but moves the cell outline to the third cell
Third `shift+enter`: Moves the cell outline to the fourth cell, but does not execute the third cell
Fourth `shift+enter`: Executes the fourth cell and prints out a new random number.

I have provided a list of my conda environment in the notebook linked above.  Let me know if I can provide any additional information! Thanks!

issue_title: edit-mode shortcuts in quickhelp are duplicated whenever quickhelp is rebuilt
issue_body: In https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/notebook/js/quickhelp.js#L260, the edit-mode shortcuts are merged into the `cm_shortcuts` array in the course of the method `build_edit_help`. However, since `cm_shortcuts` is not recreated, if `build_edit_help` is called again, edit mode shortcuts are added in again, resulting in duplicates.

issue_title: Nesting include settings in .shed.yml (source as list)?
issue_body:
Consider a moderately complex ``include`` setting like this which is quite repetitive:

```
include:
- README.rst
- venn_list.py
- venn_list.xml
- tool_dependencies.xml
- source: ../../test-data/magic.pdf
  strip_components: 2
- source: ../../test-data/venn_list.tabular
  strip_components: 2
- source: ../../test-data/rhodopsin_proteins.fasta
  strip_components: 2
```

It seems that currently ``include`` is a flat list of elements (defaulting to ``source`` entries if not a mini-dict), but allowing each ``source`` entry to be a list would allow this kind of bundling:

```
include:
- README.rst
- venn_list.py
- venn_list.xml
- tool_dependencies.xml
- strip_components: 2
  source:
  - ../../test-data/magic.pdf
  - ../../test-data/venn_list.tabular
  - ../../test-data/rhodopsin_proteins.fasta
```

e.g. Something like this hack:

```
$ git diff
diff --git a/planemo/shed/__init__.py b/planemo/shed/__init__.py
index 8bacfc8..e108bc6 100644
--- a/planemo/shed/__init__.py
+++ b/planemo/shed/__init__.py
@@ -882,7 +882,10 @@ class RealizedFile(object):
     def realized_files_for(path, include_info):
         if not isinstance(include_info, dict):
             include_info = {"source": include_info}
-        source = include_info.get("source")
+        source_list = include_info.get("source")
+        if not isinstance(source_list, list):
+            # A single filename or pattern
+            source_list = [source_list]
         destination = include_info.get("destination", None)
         strip_components = include_info.get("strip_components", 0)
         if destination is None:
@@ -890,14 +893,16 @@ class RealizedFile(object):
             destination_specified = False
         else:
             destination_specified = True
-        abs_source = os.path.join(path, source)
-        dest_is_file = destination_specified and os.path.isfile(abs_source)
         realized_files = []
-        for globbed_file in _glob(path, source):
-            src = os.path.relpath(globbed_file, path)
-            realized_files.append(
-                RealizedFile(path, src, destination, dest_is_file, strip_components)
-            )
+        for source in source_list:
+            abs_source = os.path.join(path, source)
+            dest_is_file = destination_specified and os.path.isfile(abs_source)
+            for globbed_file in _glob(path, source):
+                src = os.path.relpath(globbed_file, path)
+                realized_files.append(
+                    RealizedFile(path, src, destination, dest_is_file, strip_components)
+                )
+        info("Found %i files from source %r" % (len(realized_files), source_list))
         return realized_files
 
     def __str__(self)
```

More work would be needed to preserve the work in #158 for spotting missing includes.

Also, harking back to #160 this would be possible too (but requires a few more tweaks to ``planemo/shed/__init__.py`` in ``realize_to`` to deal with implicit destinations of ``.`` being used internally):

```
include:
- strip_components: 2
  source:
  - ../../tools/venn_list/README.rst
  - ../../tools/venn_list/venn_list.py
  - ../../tools/venn_list/venn_list.xml
  - ../../tools/venn_list/tool_dependencies.xml
  - ../../test-data/magic.pdf
  - ../../test-data/venn_list.tabular
  - ../../test-data/rhodopsin_proteins.fasta
```

Having played with this a bit, it would be an alternative solution to my proposal in #160, and might have some other future usecases - but overall I think it is too complicated?

issue_title: TODO
issue_body:
This issue consolidates todo that were listed in #36 and #38:

From #36:

 - [ ] Extend testing coverage to include the new apps and new features.

 - [x] Refactor the custom distutils command subclasses so that they are accessible for other packages -- in case they want to work around us just as we did distutils.

 - [x] Restore the default handling of generic data files so that it matches up with distutils' default behavior.  To handle stand-alone extension modules, we'll need to add extra handling logic for the `py_modules` argument; we would list these modules under `py_modules`, and leave it to scikit-build to recognize them and place them accordingly.

 - [x] Add a final check to the manifest processing loop so that skbuild refuses to handle files installed outside of the project root.

 - [ ] There are multiple bugs in the way command line arguments are processed and forwarded in scikit-build.  It's not clear if they were introduced by these changes, or if they predate them.

   - [ ] There is a bug when trying to specify the desired CMake build type via the `--build-type` option in scikit-build.  It seems that some sections of the code are looking for `CMAKE_BUILD_DIR/CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` as the ultimate location of the cmake build directory.  I think handling multiple builds in one tree is desirable and we should probably extend this handling to the other temporary staging directories, but right now, these improvements were not expecting this.  We'll need to go through the various code sections and make sure everyone's on the same page.

   - [ ] On a related note, I tried to pass options directly to CMake through scikit-build using the `--` argument seperator.  Scikit-build is currently written to expect up to three sections of such options: the first for scikit-build options, the second for CMake config options, and the third for options to the build step.  However, since CMake is used to drive the build step, options meant for the actual build tool (e.g.: make) must be separated by another `--` separator.  Currently scikit-build fails to properly handle this fourth separator.

 - [x] Add option to the `add_cython_target` function to override the variable name used to store the location to the generated source file.

 - [x] Remove TODO note in `add_cython_target` regarding C vs C++ output.  So far, no problems have been encountered one way or the other during development and testing.

 - [ ] Currently the only way to add to the Cython module search path for `add_cython_target` is to use the outdated `include_directories` CMake command.  Ideally, we would be able to use `target_include_directories` or something similar.  This may involve the creation of an actual CMake target.

 - [x] Remove the FindDebugger.cmake file (used just for debugging cmake targets).

 - [x] Update the `python_modules_header` function so that the `FORWARD_DECL_MODULES_VAR` option is renamed to `FORWARD_DECL_MODULES_LIST` and that the list in question is passed by value.

 - [x] Add option to the `python_modules_header` function to override the variable names used to store the locations of the generated header file as well as that of the directory containing it.

 - [x] Clean up tower-of-babel boost module header files.  Use `tbabel_boost_common.h` to minimize code duplication.

From #38:
 
- [ ] Extend testing coverage to include the new apps and new features.

   - [x] Projects that attempt to CMake-install files outside of its designated project installation root should fail to install.

     - [x] Ideally, they should fail prior to the CMake-install step (i.e.: before any problematic files are actually installed).  This will need code to determine the would-be files without the aid of the install manifest.

   - [x] Each of the sample projects should have their own tests to at least ensure that they build correctly, and that any produced applications run without issue.

    - [x] Add tests for pen2_cython

    - [x] Add tests for tower-of-babel

 - [ ] There are multiple bugs in the way command line arguments are processed and forwarded in scikit-build.  It's not clear if they were introduced by these changes, or if they predate them.

   - [ ] There is a bug when trying to specify the desired CMake build type via the `--build-type` option in scikit-build.  It seems that some sections of the code are looking for `CMAKE_BUILD_DIR/CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` as the ultimate location of the cmake build directory.  I think handling multiple builds in one tree is desirable and we should probably extend this handling to the other temporary staging directories, but right now, these improvements were not expecting this.  We'll need to go through the various code sections and make sure everyone's on the same page.

   - [ ] On a related note, I tried to pass options directly to CMake through scikit-build using the `--` argument seperator.  Scikit-build is currently written to expect up to three sections of such options: the first for scikit-build options, the second for CMake config options, and the third for options to the build step.  However, since CMake is used to drive the build step, options meant for the actual build tool (e.g.: make) must be separated by another `--` separator.  Currently scikit-build fails to properly handle this fourth separator.

 - [ ] Currently the only way to add to the Cython module search path for `add_cython_target` is to use the outdated `include_directories` CMake command.  Ideally, we would be able to use `target_include_directories` or something similar.  This may involve the creation of an actual CMake target.

issue_title: STACKs: Namespaces will be paid for with STACKs
issue_body:
When STACKs go live, namespaces will be purchased in STACKs.

* When sending a `NAMESPACE_PREORDER` transaction, the payer sends a dust amount to the default burn address instead of the price in Bitcoin.
* A `NAMESPACE_REVEAL` transaction is only accepted if the `NAMESPACE_PREORDER` payer address has enough STACKs.
* When a `NAMESPACE_REVEAL` transaction is committed, the `NAMESPACE_PREORDER` payer is debited the cost of the namespace in STACKs.

The namespace price curve is structured the same as it is in Bitcoin, with four price buckets.  The opening exchange rate for STACKs is 1 STACK = $0.15 USD.  The prices for namespaces in STACKs is structured to be as close as it was in September 2015, when Blockstack migrated to Bitcoin.

| Namespace length | micro-STACKs | Approx. cost in USD |
|--------------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 character | 640000000000 | $96,000 |
| 2-3 characters | 64000000000 | $9,600 |
| 4-7 characters | 6400000000 | $960 |
| 8+ characters | 640000000 | $96 |

Note that there are 1e6 micro-STACKs in 1 STACK (i.e. STACKs can subdivided into 1e6 units).

issue_title: STACKs: Implement STACKs vesting
issue_body:
When the genesis block is started, people who invested in the token sale will receive their STACKs on a vesting schedule.  `blockstackd` needs to implement vesting so that these people receive their STACKs at the right time intervals.  This also includes STACKs allocated to Blockstack BPC (which are on a different vesting schedule).

Details here: https://blockstack.com/tokenpaper.pdf

issue_title: Update the client configuration file to match the server naming scheme
issue_body:
The client and server use different configuration names for the same certificate parameters (e.g., ``certfile`` vs ``certificate_path``). This is an artifact of their separate development paths way back when. However, if a user misnames the client configuration parameters to match the server ones, the client TLS connection will fail with a non-intuitive certificate error (see #444).

Update the client configuration file to use the server naming scheme for certificate parameters, deprecating the old client names for these parameters to ensure a smooth migration period for users.

issue_title: [Decision] string representation
issue_body:
Original comment from @omangin in #64.


Then comes a new question. Currently the new parser uses a special object from `bibdatabase.py` called `BibDataString` to represent parsed string object. This, in theory, enables to delay the interpolation of bibtex strings and chose, for example, to later write it as such in a new file. It seems that the current parser by default interpolates strings and that the default interpolation content for undefined strings is the key. This means that `month = jan` is parsed into `'month': 'jan'` which would be exported as `month = {jan}` by the writer. Then I guess bibtex itself would not interpolate "jan" since it is not parsed as a string name any more.

What is yet implemented for the new parser is to interpolate known strings ("known" meaning previously defined in the bibtex file or being a common string if the option is set). This is close to the previous behavior but not exactly the same since unknown strings raise errors.

So here are the options to implement:
- the clever way: represent string expressions as such (lists of python `string` and `BibDataString`). This unables the interpolation as well as the export in the original form. This however breaks the compatibility with the current behavior. (Although it is possible to hack these *bibtex string expressions* to behave as python string.) 
- the always working solution: always interpolate. This would be fully compatible with previous behavior with the aforementioned issue.
- the strict option: fail on undefined strings, either interpolate or keep the full structure.

I think that the interpolation fits well as a customization function where I should move it. The other solutions are all easy to implement but might break some compatibility. What do you think should be the default?

Add support for rfc2579 datetime format
issue_body:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2579

These timestamps are used in ipp cache files (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2911).

```
DateAndTime ::= TEXTUAL-CONVENTION
    DISPLAY-HINT "2d-1d-1d,1d:1d:1d.1d,1a1d:1d"
    STATUS       current
    DESCRIPTION
            "A date-time specification.

            field  octets  contents                  range
            -----  ------  --------                  -----
              1      1-2   year*                     0..65536
              2       3    month                     1..12
              3       4    day                       1..31
              4       5    hour                      0..23
              5       6    minutes                   0..59
              6       7    seconds                   0..60
                           (use 60 for leap-second)
              7       8    deci-seconds              0..9
              8       9    direction from UTC        '+' / '-'
              9      10    hours from UTC*           0..13
             10      11    minutes from UTC          0..59

            * Notes:
            - the value of year is in network-byte order
            - daylight saving time in New Zealand is +13

            For example, Tuesday May 26, 1992 at 1:30:15 PM EDT would be
            displayed as:

                             1992-5-26,13:30:15.0,-4:0

```

issue_title: MIT krb5 refuses to inquire about partially established contexts

issue_title: Creation of X509Certificate2 fails on Linux but works on Windows
issue_body:
# Creation of X509Certificate2 fails on Linux but works on Windows

Creating a X509Certificate2 instance from a byte array works on Windows but fails on Linux with a "CryptographicException".

# General

Tested Systems:
Windows 10
Ubuntu 16.04, Ubuntu 17.10
OpenSuse 42

All tested with:
**Dotnet Core Version: 2.0.2**

Example code:
```
static void Main(string[] args)
{
      var cert = new X509Certificate2(Cert.CertBytes);
}
```
On Windows: Valid X509Certificate2 instance is created
On Linux: An exception is thrown:
```
{System.Security.Cryptography.CryptographicException: Cannot find the original signer.
   at Internal.Cryptography.Pal.PkcsFormatReader.TryReadPkcs7(SafePkcs7Handle pkcs7, Boolean single, ICertificatePal& certPal, List`1& certPals)
   at Internal.Cryptography.Pal.PkcsFormatReader.TryReadPkcs7Der(Byte[] rawData, Boolean single, ICertificatePal& certPal, List`1& certPals)
   at Internal.Cryptography.Pal.CertificatePal.FromBlob(Byte[] rawData, SafePasswordHandle password, X509KeyStorageFlags keyStorageFlags)
   at System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate..ctor(Byte[] data)
   at System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2..ctor(Byte[] rawData)
   at CertTest.Program.Main(String[] args) in /home/CertTest/Program.cs:line 14}
```

If the certificate is first parsed by BouncyCastle and then converted into a X509Certificate2 it works.
Example which works on Linux
```
static void Main(string[] args)
{
       var bouncy = new Org.BouncyCastle.X509.X509CertificateParser();
       var bcert = bouncy.ReadCertificate(Cert.CertBytes);
       var wcert = new System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2(bcert.GetEncoded());
}
```

issue_title: Request.Path is empty while Request.PathBase is not
issue_body:
# Request.Path is empty while Request.PathBase is not

# General

Using .NET Core 2.0, Windows 10, IIS Express.

While debugging my .NET Core web application, IIS Express launches a new browser window with the address `localhost:7777/dashboard`. Usually, I am able to view my web site at this point, but upon transferring my project over to a new machine with a fresh copy of Visual Studio Community 2017, the project returns a very strange error. The environment is identical to the environment on my last machine, so I see no reason why this error should occur. The error occurs because `HttpContext.Request.Path` is empty, even though I am accessing the path `/dashboard` in the URL. I noticed something very strange while looking into the error. `HttpContext.Request.PathBase` returns `/dashboard` even though `HttpContext.Request.Path` is returns an empty string.

The `HttpContext` object is accessed from the `context` argument passed within the line of code, `app.Run(async (context) =>{});` located in `Startup.cs`.

issue_title: [Issues on Push Message]
issue_body:
Hello, Gene

Currently I’m facing several problems when trying to implement following push message use cases in client app:
1. FCM token refresh in the middle of user session
2. User logout in app

So as far as I understand, it is possible for FCM token to be refreshed. When this event occurs, we need to update our existing token on the server.

Yet in Tinode, FCM token (called `"deviceId"` in Tinode) could only be updated in `{hi}`. So in case of token refresh occurs in the middle of user session, we need to break the session first to register new updated token. 

So maybe there is a better way to do this which I'm missing?

The next issue occurs when single device is being used by multiple user. So for example User A & User B use single device together.

In Tinode, `deviceId` is associated with `uid` [(link)](https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/store/store.go#L430-L432), yet `store.Devices.Delete()` is only called when `deviceId` is invalid [(link)](https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/push_fcm/push_fcm.go#L150).

Since User A & User B use the device together, then the `deviceId` would be same for them. So in case User A has been logout & User B logged in, then message for User A would be still delivered to User B. 

So I think it's better if we also offers delete device functionality for user so the client could notify server which `deviceId` still used for certain user & which is not.

What do you think?

---

Also if I may suggest I think it's better if we differentiate between `deviceId` & fcm token though. The reason is because essentialy a device may have multiple tokens due to rotation / refresh. 

Since basically `store.Devices.Update()` is defined as "upsert", then every unused fcm token would be just pile up on database. But if we differentiate between `deviceId` & fcm token then we could just update the associated token for the `deviceId` leaving more compact space on the database.

What do you think?

Thanks

You could see the details [here](https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/devel/server/topic.go#L439-L444), which shown topic only exclude device ids which subscribed to current topic.

I think this behavior will annoys user though. Because even though user is already online in app he would still receive notification.

What do you think?

Thanks

issue_title: produce() raises KafkaError{code=_UNKNOWN_TOPIC,val=-188,str="Unable to produce message: Local: Unknown topic"}
issue_body:
confluent-kafka==0.9.2, librdkafka 0.9.2, kafka 0.10.1.0, Python 2.7.6

I run 3 AWS instances with kafka brokers and zookepers. Each topic has 1 partition and replication factor 3. Producers (web apps) run continuously (except periodic autorestarting) on other instances and on app start each one gets bootstrap.servers set to a list of current kafka instance IPs.
Sometimes I need to recreate one of these instances. I stop kafka server, then zookeeper, then terminate the instance. ISR shrinks to 2 as expected and so on (`kafka-topics.sh --describe`). Producer gets mad though (not sure if always or only when this instance was the topic leader) and for some period raises:
```
...
    _producer.produce(topic, cPickle.dumps(value, KAFKA_PICKLE_PROTO))
KafkaException: KafkaError{code=_UNKNOWN_TOPIC,val=-188,str="Unable to produce message: Local: Unknown topic"}
```
Obviously, it results in losing these messages. How to avoid it (except catching it and retrying in a blocking way)?

The second problem. There are other combinations of accompanying errors and I'm not sure if I understand which are transient and which indicate message loss (and how to recognize it at all):
* error_cb: _TRANSPORT: some_ip:9092/bootstrap: Connect to ipv4#some_ip:9092 failed: No route to host

issue_title: Allow tzinfos to return None
issue_labels: [{'id': 149492525, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 149492528, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}, {'id': 667039516, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/low-difficulty', 'name': 'low-difficulty', 'color': '0e8a16', 'default': False}, {'id': 177049992, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/parser', 'name': 'parser', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': False}, {'id': 177050120, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dateutil/dateutil/labels/time%20zones', 'name': 'time zones', 'color': 'c7def8', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Currently, if you the result of a `tzinfos` call *explicitly* returns `None`, that will throw an error:

```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> parse('2017-02-03 12:40 MGX', tzinfos={'MGX': None})
...
ValueError: Offset must be tzinfo subclass, tz string, or int offset.
>>> parse('2017-02-03 12:40 RDT', tzinfos={'XXX': None})
[...] UnknownTimezoneWarning: tzname RDT identified but not understood.  Pass `tzinfos`
argument in order to correctly return a timezone-aware datetime.  In a future version,
this raise an exception.
  category=UnknownTimezoneWarning)
datetime.datetime(2014, 2, 3, 2, 3)
```

The whole point of #540  was that implicitly silently failing was a bad behavior, but you should certainly be able to opt in to not caring about the time zone by returning `None`.

Note: As an aside, there's a typo I didn't notice there, it says "In a future version, this raise an exception", should be "this *will* raise an exception."

issue_title: Scope of StepInput Expressions
issue_body:
I spend some hours yesterday in order to detect that StepInputExpressions:


```
inputs:
  input1: string

steps:
  step1:
    in:
      argument1:
        valueFrom: $(...)
      argument2:
        source: "#input1"
```

doesn't have access to the input variables. I was furiously trying to refer them by inputs.input1, changing everything around this, assuming the error resided elsewhere. In the end I understood I had to add the required variables as additional input. It makes total sense to have it like that. I have nothing against it, but I would wish it to be explained better in the documentation.

I read the WorkflowStepInput/Fields/valueFrom again. Knowing how it works, I can parse the definition, but I missed it earlier. 

In hindsight, this is obviously a mistake on my side. If anything it boils down to a wish for clearer user documentation, perhaps an extension of the gentle introduction to include more of the nitty-gritty details.

issue_title: Misleading error: ImproperlyConfigured: django-ckeditor requires CKEDITOR_MEDIA_PREFIX setting
issue_body:
In widgets.py there is a try block where any AttributeError that happens inside of that results in this error:

https://github.com/django-ckeditor/django-ckeditor/blob/17d89a2ef7efdf91fb266ed84031f6cf2693b07e/ckeditor/widgets.py#L61-L78

CKEDITOR_BASEPATH is what is being checked, but with a getattr and a default. CKEDITOR_MEDIA_PREFIX is not being checked at all.

In my case CKEDITOR_MEDIA_PREFIX and CKEDITOR_BASEPATH are set and I still have no idea what is wrong.

It must be something inside js_assets.

I already fixed the issue, so I don't need help, but I popped in to let you guys know there is an issue and how I solved it. 

So I configured my vimrc to use python3

``` let g:powerline_pycmd = 'py3' ```

after that I started getting an exception in my vim console, so I output it to a logfile

This was the file:
```
2018-01-23 01:40:12,716:DEBUG:vim:Using libuv-based watcher
2018-01-23 01:40:16,876:ERROR:vim:branch:Exception while computing segment: _getvolumepathname() argument 1 must be str, not bytes

    return path.rstrip(seps) == _getvolumepathname(path).rstrip(seps)
TypeError: _getvolumepathname() argument 1 must be str, not bytes
2018-01-23 01:40:16,877:ERROR:vim:file_vcs_status:Exception while computing segment: _getvolumepathname() argument 1 must be str, not bytes
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\site-packages\powerline\segment.py", line 173, in process_segment
    contents = segment['contents_func'](pl, segment_info)
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\site-packages\powerline\segment.py", line 412, in <lambda>
    contents_func = lambda pl, segment_info: _contents_func(pl=pl, segment_info=segment_info, **args)
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\site-packages\powerline\segments\vim\__init__.py", line 555, in file_vcs_status
    repo = guess(path=name, create_watcher=create_watcher)
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\site-packages\powerline\lib\vcs\__init__.py", line 236, in guess
    for directory in generate_directories(path):
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\site-packages\powerline\lib\vcs\__init__.py", line 23, in generate_directories
    if os.path.ismount(path):
  File "J:\ProgrammingStuff\ProgrammingTools\Python36\lib\ntpath.py", line 294, in ismount
    return path.rstrip(seps) == _getvolumepathname(path).rstrip(seps)
TypeError: _getvolumepathname() argument 1 must be str, not bytes
```

turns out it was an issue with python3 using bytes instead of strings for some things now...
So tl;dr I changed these lines of code and it fixed the issue on my end (blocks/lines I added are commented)

IN: .\powerline\lib\vcs\__init__.py:
```
def generate_directories(path):
	#Converts path from 'bytes' to 'str' object
	path = path.decode('utf-8')
	if os.path.isdir(path):
		#Converts path back into bytes and sends it out to wherever it needs to go.
		yield bytes(path, 'utf-8')
	while True:
		if os.path.ismount(path):
			break
		old_path = path
		path = os.path.dirname(path)
		if path == old_path or not path:
			break
		#Converts path back into bytes and sends it out to wherever it needs to go.
		yield bytes(path, 'utf-8')
```

IN: .\powerline\lib\shell.py:
```
def readlines(cmd, cwd):
	'''Run command and read its output, line by line

	:param list cmd:
		Command which will be run.
	:param str cwd:
		Working directory of the command which will be run.
	'''
	
	#Converts every 'bytes' object in cmd into a str object
	cmd = list(cmd)
	for i in range(len(cmd)):
		if type(cmd[i]) == type(bytes('', 'utf-8')):
			cmd[i] = cmd[i].decode()
	cmd = tuple(cmd)
	
	#converts cwd to 'str' object
	cwd = cwd.decode()
	
	p = Popen(cmd, shell=False, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE, cwd=cwd)
	encoding = get_preferred_input_encoding()
	p.stderr.close()
	with p.stdout:
		for line in p.stdout:
			yield line[:-1].decode(encoding)
```

Feel free to make any modifications and/or use in your codebase if you want. 👍

issue_title: ISO8601 time string with a Z comes out with +00:00 after load and then dump
issue_body:
When using the `fields.DateTime` and a value `"2018-07-25T00:00:00Z"`. Loading and then dumping that data should result in the exact same value for that field. But it doesn't. What is dumped back out is `"2018-07-25T00:00:00+00:00"`. datetime strings are really difficult with python. My sympathies.

I'm going to try to make a subclass of field that does only what I want for tz-aware iso8601 strings and isn't as broad as `fields.DateTime`. I'll post it back for reference.

issue_title: Strange behaviour when attempting to evaluate batches of sequences
issue_body:
I have the following model defined in keras which I convert to CoreML:

```
SEQUENCE_LENGTH = 8000
NUM_CATS = 1025

inputs = Input(shape=(SEQUENCE_LENGTH,))
L0 = Reshape((SEQUENCE_LENGTH,1))(inputs)
L1 = Conv1D(filters=4,
             kernel_size=4,
             strides=4, 
             padding='same', 
             activation='relu')(L0)
L2  = Dense(32, activation='relu')(L1)
L3 = Dense(NUM_CATS, activation='softmax')(L2)

model = Model(inputs=inputs, outputs=L3)
```

If I run a prediction with one sequence it appears to work correctly:

```
inputx = np.array(X[0])
print(inputx.shape)
t0 = time.time()
print("CoreML model output shape: ", coreml_model.predict({'input1': inputx})['output1'].shape)
print("Time taken: ", time.time()-t0)
```

However if I run it on multiple sequences the output shape is incorrect:

```
inputx = np.array([X[0:20]])
print(inputx.shape)
t0 = time.time()
print("CoreML model output shape: ", coreml_model.predict({'input1': inputx})['output1'].shape)
print("Time taken: ", time.time()-t0)
```

The output shape should be `(20, 2000, 1025)` not `(2000, 1025)`. The time taken to process has increased by 20x so something is happening. As things stand without the ability to process in batches evaluating this particular model on CPU is faster than GPU.

issue_title: Model Visualization: wrong output shape for flatten layer
issue_body: To reproduce the issue: call `coremltools.utils.visualize_spec(...)`on a model with a flatten layer, and click the button to turn on shapes. The output for a flatten layers always seem to be `[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]`. However it should be `[1, 1, C * H * W, 1, 1]` where `C`, `H`, and `W` are based on the input to the flatten layer.

issue_title: Best practices in avoiding circular imports?
issue_body:
My problem is that it's hard to avoid circular imports when using GenericForeignKeyField and structuring code logically.

Is there any method like Django's "get_model" but for resources? 

More or less pseudo-code example (some imports etc removed):

api/org/resource.py
```python

from api.models import Organization, StatusUpdate
from api.user.resource import UserResource
from api.statusupdate.resource import StatusUpdateResource

from .authorization import OrganizationAuthorization
from .authentication import OrganizationAuthentication

class OrganizationResource(ModelResource):
    members = fields.ToManyField(
        UserResource,
        'members',
        full=True, null=True, blank=True)
    creator = fields.ForeignKey(UserResource, 'creator', full=True)

    class Meta:
        queryset = Organization.objects.filter(disabled=False)
        resource_name = 'organization'
        always_return_data = True
        authorization = OrganizationAuthorization()
        authentication = OrganizationAuthentication()

    def prepend_urls(self):
        return [
            url(r"^(?P<resource_name>%s)/(?P<pk>\w[\w/-]*)/statuses%s$" %
                (self._meta.resource_name, trailing_slash()), self.wrap_view('get_statuses'), name="api_get_statuses"),
        ]

    def get_statuses(self, request, **kwargs):
        """
        GET /api/v1/org/1/statuses/
        """
        try:
            # Create bundle and fetch the requested object from cache
            bundle = self.build_bundle(data={'pk': kwargs['pk']}, request=request)
            obj = self.cached_obj_get(bundle=bundle, **self.remove_api_resource_names(kwargs))
        except ObjectDoesNotExist:
            return HttpGone()
        except MultipleObjectsReturned:
            return HttpMultipleChoices("More than one resource is found at this URI.")
        res = StatusUpdateResource()
        status_update_contenttype = ContentType.objects.get(
            app_label="app",
            model="statusupdate"
        )
        queryset = StatusUpdate.objects
        queryset = queryset.filter(object_id=obj.pk, content_type=status_update_contenttype)
        queryset = queryset.order_by('-created').all()
        bundles = []

        for obj in queryset:
            bundle = res.build_bundle(obj=obj, request=request)
            bundles.append(res.full_dehydrate(bundle, for_list=True))

        return self.create_response(request, bundles)
```


api/statusupdate/resource.py
```python
from tastypie.contrib.contenttypes.fields import GenericForeignKeyField
from tastypie.resources import ModelResource

from api.org.resource import OrganizationResource
from api.event.resource import EventResource

from api.models import StatusUpdate


class StatusUpdateResource(ModelResource):
    content_object = GenericForeignKeyField({
        Event: EventResource,
        Organization: OrganizationResource
    }, 'content_object')

    class Meta:
        resource_name = 'statusupdates'
        queryset = StatusUpdate.objects.all()
````

issue_title: Provide a way to print BigDL debug log without modifying code
issue_body:
Some debug log is helpful to investigate issue. eg. 
========== Metrics Summary ==========
get weights average : 
computing time average : 
send weights average : 
xxxxx

For existing examples, eg. googlenet, by default they are turn off. and currently if we want to get above log, we have to edit the code, changing logger.debug to logger.info or update log level in train.scala. It's not very convenient as some users have build bigdl into a image and if they want to get the debug log, they have to edit code, recompile it and rebuild a image. It would be better if we can provide a way to show these logs without modifying the code.

issue_title: fix linke turbidity time interpolation
issue_body:
As indicated in [in `clearsky.lookup_linke_turbidity` on L212-224](../blob/master/pvlib/clearsky.py#L212-L224)

> This is approximate and could be made more accurate.

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.linspace(-15, 380, num=14)
array([ -15.        ,   15.38461538,   45.76923077,   76.15384615,
        106.53846154,  136.92307692,  167.30769231,  197.69230769,
        228.07692308,  258.46153846,  288.84615385,  319.23076923,
        349.61538462,  380.        ])
```

Another method would be to use the calendar month days:

```python
>>> import calendar
>>> import numpy as np
>>> mdays = calendar.mdays[1:]
>>> np.concatenate([[-calendar.mdays[1] / 2.0],
                    np.cumsum(mdays) - np.array(mdays) / 2.,
                    [365 + calendar.mdays[-1] / 2.0]])
array([ -15.5,   15.5,   45. ,   74.5,  105. ,  135.5,  166. ,  196.5,
        227.5,  258. ,  288.5,  319. ,  349.5,  380.5])
```

This still doesn't account for leap years, but we can use `calendar.isleap(<year>)`:
```python
import calendar
import numpy as np


def calendar_month_middles(year):
    mdays = np.array(calendar.mdays[1:])  # copy
    ydays = 365
    if calendar.isleap(year):
        mdays[1] = mdays[1] + 1
        ydays = 366
    return np.concatenate([[-calendar.mdays[-1] / 2.0],
                           np.cumsum(mdays) - np.array(mdays) / 2.,
                           [ydays + calendar.mdays[1] / 2.0]])
```

now you can see the difference in leap year

```python
>>> calendar_month_middles(2016)  # a leap year
array([ -15.5,   15.5,   45.5,   75.5,  106. ,  136.5,  167. ,  197.5,
        228.5,  259. ,  289.5,  320. ,  350.5,  381.5])
>>> calendar_month_middles(2015)  # NOT a leap year
array([ -15.5,   15.5,   45. ,   74.5,  105. ,  135.5,  166. ,  196.5,
        227.5,  258. ,  288.5,  319. ,  349.5,  380.5])
```

Note: it's important to copy `mdays` because it's a _borrowed_ reference, so if you change it, the change is permanent.

```python
>>> calendar.mdays
[0, 31, 28, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31]
>>> calendar.mdays[3] = 100
>>> calendar.mdays
[0, 31, 28, 100, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31]
calendar_month_middles(2015)  # yikes!
array([ -15.5,   15.5,   45. ,  109. ,  174. ,  204.5,  235. ,  265.5,
        296.5,  327. ,  357.5,  388. ,  418.5,  380.5])
```

issue_title: bug in get_relative_airmass(model='youngirvine1967')
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
pvlib python implements the Young and Irvine 1967 model as:
```python
    elif 'youngirvine1967' == model:
        am = ((1.0 / np.cos(zenith_rad)) *
              (1 - 0.0012*((1.0 / np.cos(zenith_rad)) ** 2) - 1))
```

The published formula is

```
am = sec(z) * (1 - 0.0012 * (sec(z)^2 - 1))
```

See http://adsbit.harvard.edu/cgi-bin/nph-iarticle_query?bibcode=1967AJ.....72..945Y&db_key=AST&page_ind=3&plate_select=NO&data_type=GIF&type=SCREEN_GIF&classic=YES

If you squint very hard you might notice that pvlib python's implementation is equivalent to

```
am = sec(z) * (1 - 0.0012 * sec(z)^2 - 1)
```

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: 0.5.2. I probably broke it when cleaning up code in the 0.1-0.2 era.

**Additional context**
Discovered when implementing #158. Write good unit tests! I'll make a pr with a fix soon.

issue_title: Delete Relationships
The JSON API and EmberJS use dashed attributes by default
issue_body:
My problem is mainly a documentation issue, you should add `JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS = 'dasherize'` to the README and the docs.

The JSON API specification and the [EmberJS framework expect dash-cased attributes](https://guides.emberjs.com/v2.5.0/models/customizing-serializers/#toc_json-api-document], but the DRF sends by default the (PEP8) underscored attributes.

Beside mentioning the `JSON_API_FORMAT_KEYS` setting, you may concider using the dashed case by default.

issue_title: No straightforward way for bound methods to return relations.
issue_body:
Sometimes I need to get a relationship based from a function on a model like this.

    class Post(models.Model):
        # attributes and stuff
       
       def user_recommendation(self, user)
           return self.recommends.get(user=user)

 In vanila DRF it is possible to use methods like `user_recommendation` by overriding `RelatedField` or using a`SerializerMethodField`.  However neither of those methods work with DRF-JSON-API. Using a `SerializerMethodField` results in the relation being placed in attributes, not relations.

While subclassing `RelatedField` fails because at this point in the [renderer](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/develop/rest_framework_json_api/utils.py#L325) attempts to get an id from`relation_instance_or_manager`, but will fail because it's a bound method.

-----

To get the relationship of user_recommendation I have to resort to a dirty hack. Where I subclass `HyperlinkedRelatedField` or `PKRelatedField`, and override `get_attribute` like this.

    class RecommendationField(serializers.HyperlinkedRelatedField):
        def get_attribute(self, instance): 
            request = self.context['request']
            recommendation = instance.user_recommendation(request.user)
            instance.user_recommendation.__dict__['pk'] = recommendation.pk
            return recommendation


Problematically, this breaks `extract_included`.

-----

Would something along the lines of a `RelatedSerializerMethodField`, be the right solution to this?

issue_title: results attribute instead of data in response.data when testing
issue_body:
When running tests the `response.data` doesn't contain `data` key. Instead the `results` key is available.

Example code:
```
class AdvertsTestCase(APITestCase):
    def test_get_adverts_list(self):
        advert1 = f.AdvertFactory.create()
        advert2 = f.AdvertFactory.create()
        advert3 = f.AdvertFactory.create()

        url = reverse('advert-list')
        response = self.client.get(url)

        print(response.data)

        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, status.HTTP_200_OK)
```
The result from `print(response.data)`
```
{'results': [ ... ], 'meta': { ... }, 'links': { ... }}
```
But when access the `response.content` there is `data` attribute in the raw response's data:

```
b'{"links":{ ... },"data":[ ... ],"meta":{ ... }'
```

When accessing API via Postman or web-browser, the `data` key in the response is available. Seems like tests doesn't use renderers correctly - it's my assumption.

issue_title: Allow to specify Redis cluster name when using RedisNode component
issue_body:
Right now when using `RedisNode` component to create Redis instance cluster name is set to the name of the resource section where component is declared:

```yaml
SenzaComponents:
  - Configuration:
      Type: Senza::StupsAutoConfiguration
  - Redis:
      Type: Senza::RedisNode
      CacheNodeType: "{{Arguments.InstanceType}}"
      CacheParameterGroupName: "{{Arguments.RedisParameterGroupName}}"
      EngineVersion: "{{Arguments.RedisVersion}}"
      SecurityGroups:
        - Fn::GetAtt: ["RedisSecurityGroup", GroupId]
```

The above definition will create a cluster named `redis`.

While it's possible to use mustache templates to name a section I don't particulary like that approach as it hurts readability in my opinion.

I would like to propose to support `ClusterName` attribute which would allow to set cluster name, For compatibility it will fallback to using section name. After that change the template will look like this:

```yaml
SenzaComponents:
  - Configuration:
      Type: Senza::StupsAutoConfiguration
  - Redis:
      Type: Senza::RedisNode
      CacheNodeType: "{{Arguments.InstanceType}}"
      CacheParameterGroupName: "{{Arguments.RedisParameterGroupName}}"
      ClusterName: "{{Arguments.Environment}}-redis-{{Arguments.ClusterName}}"
      EngineVersion: "{{Arguments.RedisVersion}}"
      SecurityGroups:
        - Fn::GetAtt: ["RedisSecurityGroup", GroupId]
```

I will provide a PR for this ticket shortly.

issue_title: z3 installs into the non-standard dist-packages
issue_body:
z3 installs the Python bindings into dist-packages, which is non-standard (see http://stackoverflow.com/a/9388115/161801), and not in the `sys.path` by default on most platforms. Ideally z3 should be using distutils and a setup.py to install its Python bindings, but regardless, it should install to a standard location (i.e., site-packages). 

It would also be better to install z3 as a module rather than as several flat files, to avoid namespace conflicts. I don't know if this can be done without breaking the API however.
