issue_title: Improve Python bindings
issue_body:
Hi!
I just played a little with the FP support in the unstable branch (in Python), and while doing so, I noticed some things that I think could be improved:
- the `fpTo*` functions that convert FP values to something else return `FPRef`s instead of what they convert to. This is a problem because some functions and operators don't work correctly this way if for example both of their arguments are `fpToIEEEBV(foo)`
- there are `fpTo*` functions that actually convert from something to FP (like `fpToFPUnsigned`) and the naming makes this pretty confusing (something like `UBVToFp` would be much clearer, mirroring `fpToUBV`)
- this is mostly the expansion of the previous item; it would be nice if all the conversion functions would be consistently named.

That is all for now, If I notice anything else I'll add it. If I'm wrong about something, please do tell me :)

issue_title: Implement crop layer as in Caffe for semantic segmentation
issue_body:
I've been trying to implement fully convolutional networks ([fcn8s](https://github.com/shelhamer/fcn.berkeleyvision.org)), however I had some difficulty in the `crop` layer after `deconvolution`. I tried to use `SliceLayer` to perform `center`, `upper` or `lower` slicing of each axis (width and height), and the results were not satisfactory.

After checking the source of `Caffe` as [here](https://github.com/BVLC/caffe/blob/74cc4970c8f1b42056c8e36dc9944234ad663a16/python/caffe/coord_map.py), the `crop layer` takes two bottom layers (`input` and `convolution` or `deconvolution`) and outputs a single layer that matches the output shape of `input`. In addition, it performs differently for `convolution` and `deconvolution` based on their parameters, e.g., `kernel size`, `stride` and `pad`.

Can anyone give some suggestions on how to implement this in Lasagne/Theano? Many thanks in advance.

issue_title: django-ses is unable to use ECS task roles, uses instance role instead (boto3 required?)
issue_body:
Probably because django-ses is not using boto3 it can't use IAM Roles for ECS tasks feature.
Whenever it tries to send email it uses role from instance profile instead.
And if instance is not allowed to access SES we get permission errors.

IAM Roles for Tasks is described here: [http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html)

django-amazon-ses is using boto3 and uses task role correctly.

issue_title: 2 bugs in CFGAccurate
issue_body:
Hi,

Please take a look at this binary
[aes.zip](https://github.com/angr/angr/files/1450265/aes.zip)

1, We are having blocks not in their correct function. Examples are blocks from `0x93c9` to `0x946d`: they are supposed to be in `_vfprintf_r` function, but ended up being in `__aeabi_dcmpeq`. Interestingly, `__aeabi_dcmpeq` is called in block right before `0x93c9`. I guess this is a bug in https://github.com/angr/angr/pull/706?

2, Some blocks are simply missing. `__vfprintf_r` should contains block at `0xa057` which is not there. `cfg.get_any_node(0xa057` also gives None.

@ltfish hope that I've explained clearly the issue.

Thanks

issue_title: Extraction secrets from a rooted Android doesn't work properly
issue_body:
I accidentally found that function ```extract_secrets_from_android_rooted``` actually doesn't extract anything and often raising ```RuntimeError```. Here is the output of function calling:
```python
RuntimeError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-c29d59caae58> in <module>()
----> 1 steam.guard.extract_secrets_from_android_rooted()

/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/steam/guard.py in extract_secrets_from_android_rooted(adb_path)
    483
    484     if data[0] != "{":
--> 485         raise RuntimeError("Got invalid data: %s" % repr(data))
    486
    487     return {int(x['steamid']): x

RuntimeError: Got invalid data: b'* daemon not running. starting it now on port 5037 *\n* daemon started successfully *\n{"steamguard_scheme...}"
```
```data``` contains 3 strings if adb server wasn't running before function call and 1 string if adb server is running:
```python
RuntimeError: Got invalid data: b'{"steamguard_scheme":"2","steamid":"123","shared_secret":"***=","status":1,"revocation_code":"<>","secret_1":"***=","server_time":"123","identity_secret":"***=","uri":"otpauth:\\/\\/totp\\/login?secret=***=&issuer=Steam","token_gid":"123","serial_number":"123","account_name":"login"}'
```
In my opinion function may be this:
```python
def extract_secrets_from_android_rooted(adb_path='adb'):
    data = subprocess.check_output([
        adb_path, 'shell', 'su', '-c',
        "'cat /data/data/com.valvesoftware.android.steam.community/files/Steamguard*'"
        ],  universal_newlines=True) # Add 'universal_newlines' argument to get text string instead of binary
    # If adb daemon running after this command, there will be more than 1 string, we need last 
    if data.find('\n') != -1:
        data = data.split('\n')[-1]
    try:
       # We don't need to validate data manually, so we can just parse it by json module
        return json.loads(data)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        raise RuntimeError("Got invalid data: %s" % repr(data))
```
Or something like that. 
And we can extract device id from android. This data is contained in this file: ```/data/data/com.valvesoftware.android.steam.community/shared_prefs/steam.uuid.xml```, but I don't know how to parse xml.

issue_title: Реализовать магические методы для базовых классов
issue_body:
Например, в https://github.com/bureaucratic-labs/yargy/commit/b88aba6a917a3c9606e37ada71738d925e5ede04 уже реализован метод `__or__` для класса `Rule`, что дает возможность записать правила так:
```python
A = rule(...)
B = rule(...)
C = (A | B).interpretation(object)
```
Сейчас такое же правило будет выглядеть так:
```python
A = rule(...)
B = rule(...)
C = or_(A, B).interpretation(object)
```
Нужно посмотреть, какие методы можно реализовать (например, `__and__` для конструкций вида `(A & B) == and_(A, B)` и `__invert__` для `~(A & B) == not_(and_(A, B))`)

API to get information back from specific commands
issue_body:
At the moment we have attributes and some methods that let us query specific information from the vehicle (home position, location, gps etc). We can send MAVLink messages that "do" something - set Yaw, move to position, set servo value, etc. 

What we don't have is a public method to get arbitrary information back from the vehicle. So for example I can sent a message to the API with the message ID requesting the camera status or the current value of a particular servo, but I can't then get the response back into my application.

In particular it would be great to be able to query the capabilities of a particular target platform.

issue_title: Flexible and extended configuration file
issue_body:
I would like to suggest a more flexible yaml configuration.

1) Allow every property at the root level, as well as the profile level. Do not require any property at the root level, as long as it is provided at the profile level.

2) Allow the specification of user, password and host in the yaml file

Following is a sample yaml file, and its execution

```yaml
profiles:
  dev:
    keyspace: schema_dev
    host: localhost
    user: dev_user
    password: dev_pwd
  dev:
    keyspace: schema_prod
    host: db.my.dmain
    user: prod_user
migrations_path: .
replication:
  class: SimpleStrategy
  replication_factor: 3
```

```bash
cassandra-migrate migrate
cassandra-migrate --profile prod --password prod_pwd migrate
```

Thanks for cassandra-migrate.

issue_title: Do not over-catch exceptions.
issue_body:
Is there a reason to catch all exceptions on each request as in https://github.com/UMIACS/rgwadmin/blob/1.1.2/rgwadmin/rgw.py#L132-L134 ?

The way all responses are normalized to `None` whatever happens doesn't help. There's no way to make the difference between an empty returned value of a regular request, and one that return nothing because it's failing, due to missing capabilities, network error or any internal issue.

What I suggest is to simply let the request raise an exception on error, and let the caller decide of an appropriate action. Exceptions are cheap in Python. Let's use and abuse them ! :smiley_cat:

What's your opinion @dyarnell ?

issue_title: Using plugin alias that doesn't match click.group method name
issue_body:
(Sorry that this is probably a bit more of a help question than an issue, but it didn't look like there was much activity on SO for click-plugins ...)

I'm trying to register a click group as both a console script and a click plugin.  Assuming my CLI code for the plugin looks like this:
```
import click

@click.group()
def main_group():
    """
    Root plugin group
    """

@main_group.command(
    'subcommand_1',
    short_help='Subcommand #1'
)
def subcommand_1():
    print('This is subcommand #1')
```
and the `entry_points` section of `setup.py` looks like this:
```
entry_points='''
    [console_scripts]
    foo=root_plugin.cli:main_group
        
    [root.plugins]
    plugin=root_plugin.cli:main_group
'''
```
When I run the main root console script, I get this:
```
Usage: root [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  Root command line interface

Options:
  --version  Show the version and exit.
  --help     Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  main_group  Root plugin group
```
I was expecting that `main_group` would have been called `plugin` instead based on the entry point.  Note that using the `foo` console script works as expected.  In the examples I've seen, the plugin alias always matches the click group name, e.g. [here](https://github.com/click-contrib/click-plugins/blob/master/example/PrintItStyle/setup.py#L18-L19), but is it possible to give it a different alias?

issue_title: GaudiRun support for complex splitting of jobs
issue_body:
I'm still not certain how best to proceed with supporting the workflow of sending unique files per inputfile with each subjob.

I'm assuming that at some level there is the same inputdata being run over (if any) and in my mind this most likely means checking support for IGangaFile objects in the inputfiles within the GenericSplitter.

This has been reported working but it's worth formalising an example JDL and maybe making a unittest around it for this splitter.

At the same time I think it's worth exploring support for ArgSplitter as users may wish to run over the same inputdata with different conditions which are configured through some command-line arguments to their code?
(This is potentially more complex and I'm not sure how to go about supporting this for now, especially in the case of GaudiPython like behaviour where I may have to pass the sys.argc/sys.argv through to the user script which is executed depending how Python internally deals with execfile).


<Aside>
I can imagine a user wanting to change some inputfile and then run a re-processing, but I'm wondering if a user would do something to the extent of submitting many _near-identical_ jobs which may want to be individually run over a large(ish) subset of data.
I'm thinking of some sort of training exercise where a user may wish to run with 3 or 4 different scenarios over maybe ~50LFN in the inputdata which would produce ~150-200 subjobs total.
I'm wondering if there is some nice way to support this in a new type of splitter or not.
i.e. for each inputdata LFN perform multiple subjobs configured as so and collect them all into 1 master job.
aka:
`job.splitter=MyDataGroupSpltter(secondary_splitter=GenericSplitter(someArgs))`
This would pass the master job with all of the options (without inputdata) to the secondary splitter and the returned subjobs are then duplicated such that 1 set of subjobs exists for every LFN in the inputdata is created.
</Aside>

issue_title: Remove additional load after flushing an object to disk
issue_body:
When an object is flushed to disk the object is then immediately loaded back into memory and overloads itself.

This is an expensive operation and I'm not seeing a very good motivation for this to being performed on every single flush so I'm opting to have this removed.

issue_title: Add a persistent reflection cache option
issue_body:
As discussed in https://github.com/sqlalchemy-redshift/sqlalchemy-redshift/issues/101, reflection on Redshift can be time-consuming because the per-query overhead is high and SQLAlchemy issues new queries for every table you need to reflect.

I'd love to find a way to "freeze" the schema cache and have it persist while you reflect multiple tables. We already do bulk queries to get info about all tables on reflection, but that info gets thrown away after each table is reflected.

One possibility is to introduce a `RedshiftDialect._global_reflection_cache` member that would normally be `None` and modify the `reflection.cache` decorator to check for it. We'd need to be very intentional about how this gets used.

It might be nice to wrap it up in a context manager. You could write something like:
```
with RedshiftDialect.caching_schema():
    # reflect a bunch of tables here, utilizing the persistent cache

# Reflection is back to normal now that we've left the caching_schema context
```

I've never used alembic. I wonder if there's some way we could hook this into alembic so that migrations could be faster.

[Question] Use Manual Calibration Values for Registration
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | R200 | 
| Firmware Version |  1.0.72.10 | 
| Operating System & Version |  Windows 10 |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |   |
| Build System |  VS2015 | 

I need to know if it is possible to use self-calibrated values with librealsense (or modify it in a way that would use the supplied calibration matrices, not the ones stored on the device itself).
For my application, a very precise calibration is needed because it is important to get very good registration between the depth and the RGB data. The current data I'm getting does not look very good around the edges of the objects.

As I understand it, the depth is a stream of its own, computed with the ASIC on the device itself (much like Kinect's depth stream). However, with the Kinect, finding the extrinsics between the RGB and the virtual depth camera is straightforward, as you can do a standard stereo calibration between the IR camera and the RGB camera. The extrinsics found between the IR and the RGB are the same as the extrinsics between the RGB and the virtual depth camera.

On my R200, I can see that the extrinsics between the virtual depth camera and "Infrared1" is identity (that is, it is assumed that the virtual depth camera is located at the position of "infrared1"). However, there is a non-identity extrinsics matrix between "infrared1" and "infrared2". My first question is, can I assume this is by design and the virtual depth camera is always taken to be at the position of "infrared1" physical camera?

Now my second question: is there a way to tell the camera to use my manual calibration values for creating the depth stream? (As in saying "Hey R200, could you not use what you have stored on your device memory and use what I give you?") 😄 

I tried the calibration tool on Intel's website. It tells me my calibration is fine, but I based on what I saw, I think the tool is a bit sketchy and is doing a similar thing to the plane fitting approach of Daniel Herrera (from the Kinect calibration [toolbox](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/linkexchange/links/2882-kinect-calibration-toolbox)), and based on my experience, that method sucks badly for my particular application and does not produce a good enough result.

Any help is appreciated!

issue_title: librealsense always claims all connected cameras
issue_body:
Hi,

in our application it would make sense to have two separate processes using two separate cameras. This is currently not possible with librealsense, since it always opens all cameras such that the second process gets the error:

```
RealSense error calling rs_create_context(api_version:4):
    libusb_claim_interface(...) returned LIBUSB_ERROR_BUSY
```

I understand that this is not easy to solve, since it seems that librealsense actually needs to claim the usb interface in order to determine the serial number of the device.
Releasing the interface between the query and the actual usage of the device could allow a second process to claim the unused device.

Ideally, the RealSense devices would export their serial number as the USB "iSerial" attribute - then userspace applications could see that as sysfs attribute and could quickly locate the correct device without communicating with each one. However, iSerial is always 0 for our RealSense SR300 devices (see `lsusb -v` output).

Cheers,
  Max

issue_title: HttpParserUpgrade raised after event hooks are called.
issue_body:
The `HttpParserUpgrade` exception appears to get called after the event hooks have been called into (at least with small requests that fit into a single data_received call)

This makes it not terribly useful, as we may have already called into a handler function / issued a response / etc... by the time the upgrade is raised.

Sketch of protocol class:

    def __init__(self, ...):
        self.request_parser = httptools.HttpRequestParser(self)
        ...

        # The asyncio.Protocol hooks...
        ...

        def data_received(self, data):
            print('data_received()')
            try:
                self.request_parser.feed_data(data)
            except httptools.HttpParserUpgrade:
                print('upgrade exception')

        # Event hooks called back into by HttpRequestParser...
        ...

        def on_headers_complete(self):
            print('on_headers_complete()')

        def on_message_complete(self):
            print('on_message_complete()')

Output:

```
data_received()
on_headers_complete()
on_message_complete()
upgrade exception
```

On first sight this looks awkward to resolve, so it could just be considered a constraint of the implementation, and deal with it in python-land instead (which would be acceptable from my POV)

issue_title: Drop Python 2 support
issue_body:
Python 2.7 support have been extended until 2020 but there are some people panning already to move away from Python 2 and leverage Python 3's power.

The Python 3 statement website links some resources that can explain why dropping Python 2 support is a good thing. For Pulp Smash since it is intended to be run on one machine (probably a QE or developer machine) and test a Pulp server on another we are safe to drop Python 2 support because most Linux distributions come with Python 3 as the default Python interpreter.

Also by doing that we can start thinking on how to leverage Python 3 features in order to make Pulp Smash a better testing framework for Pulp.

Also we need to have some dependencies because we are needing some features that Python 3 modules already offers, for example, mock and unittest2 packages are needed because features like mock and unittest.subTests are needed and they are Python 3 builtin.

Other requirements: packaging, plumbum, python-dateutil, pyxdg and requests already supports Python 3.

The immediate gains on dropping Python 2 are to remove the overhead on making Pulp Smash runs on both Python 2 and Python 3 which will give more time to developers to add more features and test cases and drop some compatibility and packages needed to ensure Pulp Smash working on both Python versions.

[1] https://python3statement.github.io/

issue_title: Max body size units
issue_body:
I have a question regarding the `SILKY_MAX_REQUEST_BODY_SIZE` and `SILKY_MAX_RESPONSE_BODY_SIZE` settings.

The README example is following:

```
SILKY_MAX_REQUEST_BODY_SIZE = -1  # Silk takes anything <0 as no limit
SILKY_MAX_RESPONSE_BODY_SIZE = 1024  # If response body>1024kb, ignore
```

From that comment I inferred that the unit of the setting should be kB, However then I took a look at the source code and `sys.getsizeof` is used to get the size of the bodies. Doesn't that function return number of bytes and not number of kilobytes? 

In what unit should the setting be set? Kilobytes or bytes? In anycase I'd be happy to send a PR making this clearer in the README or updating the comparison in the ModelFactories to take the setting as kB...

issue_title: Need clarification of purpose for `pure_c` option
issue_body:
The description of this option is here: 

> pure_c: (Default True) If your project is C++, pass the pure_c=False, it will add new build combinations using libstdc and libstdc++11 in the `setting compiler.libcxx`.

By default, `setting compiler.libcxx` is not used for hashing/ABI compatibility, so binaries built with `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc` and `setting compiler.libcxx=libstdc++11` are identical, correct? 

I believe the primary purpose of conan-package-tools is to build the wide range of *different* binaries, so that users don't have to re-build themselves.  Since setting `pure_c=False` effectively doubles the number of builds on Linux and OSX, but half of the binaries are identical by default, then it would seem to be a bit redundant/wasteful to use this option. 

I can see one possible value proposition for this however, which is testing.  If someone wants to build their packages against both `stdc++` libraries under various compilers to make sure it works, then this makes sense.  Is this the primary purpose of exposing this option in conan-package-tools, or have I got something else wrong?

issue_title: Add support for default test categories
issue_body:
Currently, tests that are not categorized are always executed. Thus, we cannot limit tests to just a single category.

Making this happen will require a more invasive integration of the categorization logic.

One idea is to limit the implementation of this idea to a NOSE plugin.

issue_title: Add a custom summary op for PR curves that takes raw precision, recall, TP, FP, TN, FN.
issue_body:
An internal team has a custom way of computing PR curves. For their use case, true positives are rare, so  the current PR curves plots produced by TensorBoard are noisy for them.

To solve this problem, they made a streaming metric that accumulates weighted (TP, FP, TN, FN) counts across a certain window of recent steps, which has a smoothing effect and makes the PR curves jump less from step to step.

To accommodate that, I am thinking about adding a raw_precision_recall_op method to the summary.py file for PR curves. I started this issue to get everyone's input on that.

@wchargin @jart @dandelionmane

issue_title: Spinel: confusing overload of Interface Identifier term
issue_body:
The choice of the name for the "Interface Identifier" (IID) field in the Spinel header could lead to confusion.

_IP Version 6 Addressing Architecture_ [RFC 4291] defines that term, "Interface Identifier" (also abbreviated IID), to signify the 64-bit remainder of a global scope unicast address after the subnet prefix.

In IPv6 terms, this field in the Spinel header is more closely related to the "zone index" concept described in Section 6 _Zone Indices of IPv6 Scoped Address Architecture_ [[RFC 4007](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4007)], except its scope is limited to the communication channel between an OS its Spinel couterpart NCP.

I wonder if renaming this field as Zone Index Number (ZIN) would be helpful in avoiding this confusion.

issue_title: Statuts update on Incremental and Grid Search
issue_body:
Quick status update: I wanted to explore a workflow that would use scikit-learn
as much as possible. We'd use scikit-learn for all the hyper-parameter
optimization and the actual training. Dask would just provide the large
arrays.

The end goal is something as close as possible to `GridSearchCV(SGDClassifier())` trained on a larger-than-memory dataset.

```python
X, y = load_dask_arrays()

clf = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

First, we have to avoid passing a large Dask array to `SGDClassifier.fit`, as it
would be converted to an ndarray. So we wrap it with `Incremental`, which passes
blocks to the `SGDClassifier.partial_fit`:

```python
import dask_ml.wrappers

X, y = load_dask_arrays()

clf = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
inc = dsak_ml.wrappers.Incremental(clf)
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

At this point, `inc.fit(X, y)` works fine (I'm currently debugging unexpectedly
high memory usage, but let's ignore that for now), but there's a subtle issue with
`inc.score(X, y)`, which surfaces in `GridSeachCV`. By default, a pass-through
scorer is used, which is `SGDClassifier.score`. This ends up using
`sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score`, which end up converting the test Dask arrays
into large ndarrays on a single worker. This is captured in #200.

A workaround to the scoring issue is to manually pass a scorer that is able to
work well with Dask arrays. We've implemented a few in Dask-ML:

```python
import dask_ml.wrappers
import dask_ml.metrics
from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer

X, y = load_dask_arrays()
scorer = make_scorer(dask_ml.metrics.accuracy_score)

clf  = sklearn.linear_model.SGDClassifier()
inc = dask_ml.wrappers.Incremental(clf, scoring=scorer)
gs = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(clf, param_grid)
gs.fit(X, y)
```

This gets us serial hyper-parameter optimization on larger-than-memory Dask
arrays. To do things in parallel, we can use the distributed backend.


```python
import dask_ml.joblib
from sklearn.externals import joblib


with joblib.parallel_backend("dask"):
    gs.fit(X, y)
```

I'll post benchmarks later when I've run them.

issue_title: Training on selected subset of Dask DataFrame rows
issue_body:
In order to do CV iterations, I'd like to load all the data into a Dask DataFrame, select a subset of rows, and then train only on those selected rows. I've written some code that does this and it works when I use a ```LocalCluster```. When I try and use a true distributed cluster, I get an error when constructing the worker_map:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/proth/.conda/envs/dask/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dask_xgboost/core.py", line 116, in _train
    worker_map[first(workers)].append(key_to_part_dict[key])
  File "/home/proth/.conda/envs/dask/lib/python3.5/site-packages/toolz/itertoolz.py", line 368, in first
    return next(iter(seq))
StopIteration
```

I tried to write a test that demonstrates this behavior, but the test passes. This makes me think that ```distributed.utils_test.gen_cluster``` uses a ```LocalCluster```. Is there anyway to run this test with a true distributed cluster?

```
@gen_cluster(client=True, timeout=None)
def test_subset(c, s, a, b):
    # Train in xgboost with selected data
    dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(df[df.x < 8], label=labels[:7])
    bst = xgb.train(param, dtrain)

    # Combine data and labels and select rows together
    newdf = df.copy()
    newdf["labels"] = labels
    ddf = dd.from_pandas(newdf, npartitions=4)
    ddf_trimmed = ddf[ddf.x < 8]

    # Split data and labels back apart
    ddf_train = ddf_trimmed.drop("labels", axis=1)
    ddf_labels = ddf_trimmed["labels"]

    # Train in dask-xgboost with trimmed data (fails with distributed cluster!)
    dbst = yield dxgb._train(c, param, ddf_train, ddf_labels)

    result = bst.predict(dtrain)
    dresult = dbst.predict(dtrain)

    correct = (result > 0.5) == labels[:7]
    dcorrect = (dresult > 0.5) == labels[:7]
    assert dcorrect.sum() == correct.sum()
```

issue_title: ABI incompatibility between 3.5.0/3.5.1 and 3.5.2+
issue_body:
Here's the problem: if you build a pybind11 when using 3.5.2 headers, it will reference this symbol:

```sh
U _PyThreadState_UncheckedGet
```

because of how [`get_thread_state_unchecked`](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/cast.h#L130) is implemented. If you try to import this module with 3.5.0/3.5.1 version of Python, you will first get a linkage error and then most likely a segfault:

```sh
ImportError: foo.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so: undefined symbol: _PyThreadState_UncheckedGet
```

Now, this is not very cool, since ABI compatibility between micro versions is something to be expected by default. It gets much worse if you use `conda`: it differentiates package versions by the major+minor numbers in the version only, so you have packages like `foo-py34-0.1.0` and `foo-py35-0.1.0` and it's implicitly assumed they should work well for all micro versions of the interpreter.

issue_title: What is the correct way to bind copy constructor when class has Python`trampoline`?
issue_body:
Dear Pybind11 developers,

   I am trying to create bindings for copy-constructor for non-abstract class that have Python trampoline structure. What is the correct way to do that?

  For class `A` I have tried:
```cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A const &o){ return new A(o); }, [](A_trampoline const &o){ return new A_trampoline(o); } )```

 but that produce compilation error. So instead i just bind two 'copy-like' constructors:

```
cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A const &o){ return new A(o); } );
cl.def( pybind11::init( [](A_trampoline const &o){ return new A_trampoline(o); } )
```
  Is this the right way to do this?

Thanks,

issue_title: Creating a deployment
google_calendar implementation adds junk data to token files
issue_body:
At some point vdirsyncer appends extra data to the end of the generated token
file. It seems it is based of the original string, but it gets some extra
chars. Currently it has appended an extra `}` at the end which means it doesn't
parse as proper JSON. I have had other instances with some other characters of
the end of the string repeated at the very end.

I have a script that runs `vdirsyncer sync` as a background task, so I am not
sure at what point this happens. If I fix the token file manually it will work
for the several next runs and I will end up noticing this error a few days
later when I start wondering why my calendar is not synced. Then when I
run `vdirsyncer sync` I see this issue.

This might be related to running two accounts using `google_calendar` with token files. But I have not verified this.

* vdirsyncer version 0.10.1.dev48+ng139182e
* Linux 4.5.4-1-ARCH
* Python 3.5.1

config file:
```ini
[general]
status_path = ~/.vdirsyncer/status/

[pair work_calendar]
conflict_resolution = b wins
a = work_calendar_local
b = work_calendar_remote
collections = ["from a", "from b"]
metadata = ["color", "displayname"]

[storage work_calendar_local]
type = filesystem
path = ~/.calendars/work/
fileext = .ics

[storage work_calendar_remote]
type = google_calendar
token_file = ~/.vdirsyncer/google-token-work
client_id = ***
client_secret = ***

[pair personal_calendar]
conflict_resolution = b wins
a = personal_calendar_local
b = personal_calendar_remote
collections = ["from a", "from b"]
metadata = ["color", "displayname"]

[storage personal_calendar_local]
type = filesystem
path = ~/.calendars/personal/
fileext = .ics

[storage personal_calendar_remote]
type = google_calendar
token_file = ~/.vdirsyncer/google-token-personal
client_id = ***
client_secret = ***
```

output:
```
$ vdirsyncer -vdebug sync

issue_title: Please add an HPKP compatible renew option
issue_labels: [{'id': 563235901, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20csr', 'name': 'area: csr', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 261409142, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20renewal', 'name': 'area: renewal', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954789, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/feature%20request', 'name': 'feature request', 'color': '00ccff', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Please add support for renewing certificates generated using the --csr option. This is absolutely essential for HPKP implementations.

I realise this has been discussed before, but none of the alternative options are really suitable (using the certonly command with --keep doesn't work, for example).

issue_title: certbot and cron not quiet in the logs
issue_body:
Hello,
Working on Debian Jessie with certbot 0.9.3-1~bpo8+2, I noticed that certbot is no longer quiet when it is launched every 12h through CRON:
Feb 10 00:00:01 systemd[1]: Starting Certbot...
Feb 10 00:41:37 systemd[1]: Started Certbot.
Feb 10 12:00:01 systemd[1]: Starting Certbot...
Feb 10 12:29:38 systemd[1]: Started Certbot.

The relevant line in cron is:
`0 */12 * * * root test -x /usr/bin/certbot -a \! -d /run/systemd/system && perl -e 'sleep int(rand(3600))' && certbot -q renew`

How can I instruct certbot not to log these ?

Thanks

issue_title: Work around failed challenges
issue_body: If a challenge fails within the auth_handler, it would be nice to attempt to work around it.  For instance, the user might not know the recovery_token or mistakenly typed it in.  We can either try a recovery contact or a POP challenge in this case (depending on the different authenticator's capabilities/offered challenges).

issue_title: Log whether renewing or obtaining a new certificate
issue_body: It appears we don't log whether we're renewing an existing certificate or obtaining a new one. We should do this as it makes our logs more useful for debugging.

issue_title: Certbot installer has very large memory requirements
issue_body:
We're getting periodic reports that people on small-memory systems are unable to complete the installation process (using `certbot-auto`) because they get out-of-memory errors. (This is most common on a Raspberry Pi, but we've also gotten these reports from people using a memory-limited VPS configuration.)

This seems somewhat unreasonable to me; I don't understand why our resource requirements are so great.

Currently there is no way to "close" a Salesforce object/connection without explicitly deleting the Salesforce object instance. It seems like the Salesforce class would be a prime candidate to implement the context manager interface and support the use of `with` statements.

issue_title: Better identifier of temp scripts in `.sos`
issue_body:
Currently the exported temp scripts have file name `stepname_N1_N2`. However I've run into situations with multiple nested workflow, in the flavor of but much more complex than:  

```
[1]
sos_run('A:1+B:1')
[2]
sos_run('A:1+B:2')
...
```
what happen is that the file name `stepname_N1_N2` will get overwritten. It can be very annoying when errors occurred yet the pipeline did not stop immediately, and once it stopped it points to an updated version of `stepname_N1_N2` asking the user to run / debug, but in fact there is nothing wrong with that version of script. Can we use a better ID for these scripts that we can ensure that they are unique during the entire execution?

issue_title: SubFactories still save with build method (Sqlalchemy)
issue_body:
When calling build on a SQLAlchemyModelFactory, the SubFactories are still saved to the database.

```
class SqlFactory(factory.alchemy.SQLAlchemyModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        sqlalchemy_session = db.session
        sqlalchemy_session_persistence = 'commit'
```
```
BookFactory(SqlFactory):
    class Meta:
         model = Book
    name = Faker('word')
    author = SubFactory(AuthorFactory)
```
```
data = {'name': 'hello darkness'}
book = BookFactory.build(**data)
```

book is now an unsaved sqlalchemy instance as expected, but a author entry has been added to the database and is now a sqlalchemy instance that has been saved.

I would expect build() not to save/persist sub properties. If this is indeed intended behavior, any advice on how to modify the code so that author is not saved but instantiated would be great.

Thanks

issue_title: `FuzzyDate` does not return same date when seed is set
issue_body:
`FuzzyDate` takes a `start_date` and an `end_date` argument -- the `end_date` being optional.  When no `end_date` is provided, `datetime.date.today()` is used as the default value. 

When using `factory_boy` with a specific seed, one would expect anything that uses `FuzzyDate` to return the same date, each time.  But, when the code is run on two different days, the `end_date` is different because of the default value being different.  This causes the randomly generated dates to be off. 

Here is a [simplified version](https://gist.github.com/punchagan/6d2b1535726f0d917a387dd7e88d2047) of `FuzzyDate`'s code to explain the bug.

issue_title: Length of iterator fails in Python 2
issue_body: The division `len(dataset) / batch_size` will be cast to int in python2, so that `math.ceil` doesn't really work.

issue_title: A batch object created by fromvars does not have "fields" attribute
issue_body:
When making a batch object, the value of the `fields` attribute is set in its `__init__` method.
However, when created with `fromvars` class method, `fields` attribute is not set since the method first creates an empty object and then add information.
It should be modified to be analogous with the one created by `__init__` method.
It can be simply done by adding the following after https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data/batch.py#L36:
```
batch.fields = dataset.fields.keys()
```

This kind of object creation is found when using BPTT iterator. Without `fields` attribute, printing a batch object is not possible due to https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data/batch.py#L49.

issue_title: LD_LIBRARY_PATH not set on workers on different machine.
issue_body:
Starting Ray on a cluster of 3 nodes (Ubuntu 16), I encountered the following.

```python
import ray
import os

ray.init(redis_address="...")

@ray.remote
def f():
  node_ip_address = ray.services.get_node_ip_address()
  if "LD_LIBRARY_PATH" in os.environ:
    return os.environ["LD_LIBRARY_PATH"], node_ip_address
  return "", node_ip_address

results = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(1000)])
```

Then `set(results)` looked something like this

```
{('', '172.31.16.145'),
 ('', '172.31.29.31'),
 ('/usr/local/lib64:/usr/local/lib64::/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/openravepy/_openravepy_', '172.31.38.127')}
```

So locally the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` was set for each worker, but on the remote machines it was not.

The reason is that parallel-ssh does not run `source ~/.bashrc` so a bunch of environment variables are not set. We should change the cluster documentation instructions to run `source ~/.bashrc` in the script that starts Ray on the workers.

By the way, the symptom of this problem was that a remote function definition failed to be defined on the workers on the remote machines because it needed something on the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`. The actual error message (when defining the remote function) was something like

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ray-0.0.1-py2.7.egg/ray/worker.py", line 1022, in fetch_and_register_remote_function
    function = pickling.loads(serialized_function)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 1388, in loads
    return Unpickler(file).load()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 864, in load
    dispatch[key](self)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.py", line 1139, in load_reduce
    value = func(*args)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/cloudpickle/cloudpickle.py", line 717, in subimport
    __import__(name)
  File "/home/ubuntu/dex-net/deps/meshpy/meshpy/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    import meshrender
ImportError: libboost_numpy.so: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

Note: I just tried starting Ray on the non-head nodes by using

```
parallel-ssh -h workers.txt -P -I < start_worker.sh
```

where `start_worker.sh` manually sets the `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` via

```
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/lib64:/usr/local/lib64::/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/openravepy/_openravepy_
ray/scripts/start_ray.sh --num-workers=40 --redis-address=172.31.38.127:6666
```

and that solved the problem.

However, if instead of manually setting `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, I instead do `source /home/ubuntu/.bashrc`, that somehow does not help. I'm not sure why.

issue_title: Ship remote function definitions lazily when they are first invoked.
issue_body:
We may want to ship remote function definitions when they are first invoked (as opposed to when they are first defined. This would help avoid problems like #1607.

This could also avoid shipping a ton of remote function definitions that are not used (e.g., because they are defined in a Python module which is imported by a user).

The main downside is that it increases latency for the first invocation.

cc @ludwigschmidt

#### Code
```
"""Example of a custom gym environment. Run this for a demo."""

from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

import numpy as np
import gym
from gym.spaces import Discrete, Box
from gym.envs.registration import EnvSpec

import ray

from ray.tune.registry import register_env


class SimpleCorridor(gym.Env):
    """Example of a custom env in which you have to walk down a corridor.
    You can configure the length of the corridor via the env config."""

    def __init__(self, config):
        self.end_pos = config["corridor_length"]
        self.cur_pos = 0
        self.action_space = Discrete(2)
        self.observation_space = Box(
            0.0, self.end_pos, shape=(1,), dtype=np.float32)
        self._spec = EnvSpec("SimpleCorridor-{}-v0".format(self.end_pos))

    def reset(self):
        self.cur_pos = 0
        return [self.cur_pos]

        assert action in [0, 1]
        if action == 0 and self.cur_pos > 0:

        elif action == 1:

        done = self.cur_pos >= self.end_pos
        return [self.cur_pos], 1 if done else 0, done, {}

issue_title: Tasks not being executed when a sufficiently large number of tasks are scheduled remotely.
issue_body:
@atumanov uncovered the following issue.

On a 2-node cluster, start the head node with 0 CPUs, and start the other node normally.

On the head node:

```
./scripts/start_ray.sh --head --redis-port=6379 --num-cpus=0
```

On the remote node:

```
./scripts/start_ray.sh --redis-address=<HEAD-NODE-IP:6379>
```

On the head node, connect to the cluster and launch 200000 tasks.

```python
import ray

ray.init(redis_address="<HEAD-NODE-IP:6379>")

@ray.remote
def f():
  pass

l = [f.remote() for _ in range(200000)]
```

If you do this, often not all of the tasks are executed. You can see this as follows.

If you look at the task table, e.g.,

```python
r = ray.worker.global_worker.redis_client
task_ids = r.keys("TT:*")
task_info = [r.hgetall(task_id) for task_id in task_ids]
task_states = [info[b"state"] for info in task_info]  # This line takes maybe 20 seconds.

# Look at counts of different states.
[(state, task_states.count(state)) for state in set(task_states)]
```

All tasks should be in the DONE state `16`, but sometimes a ton of them are in the SCHEDULED state `2` or even the RUNNING state `8`, but nothing is in fact happening. It's almost as if the local scheduler (on the head node)'s pubsub connection to Redis which listens for tasks assigned to it has died (or something like that).

I added a CHECK which checks to make sure the publish command in the redis module that assigns a task to a local scheduler is actually received by the local scheduler (we can do this because PUBLISH returns an integer equal to the number of subscribers that received the message). And this CHECK failed at one point in the computation, so I think we're FINALLY hitting the point where Redis pubsub is dropping messages (and once it drops one message, it may just kill that pubsub connection.

issue_title: Add link to GitHub from documentation.
Incorrect Access of Dictionaries in Shared Memory
issue_body:
### System information:
- Linux Ubuntu 18.04
- Ray installed through pip3
- Ray version: 0.4.0
- Python version: 3.6.5
- Num CPUs: 12; GPUs: 1 (but GPU unused here)
- (Code shown below)

### Describe the problem
Accessing a dictionary in the object store keys incorrectly sometimes; particularly, it appears, on the first access after slices of object store arrays are stored in member variables of a class, though I have not checked the boundaries here of where else the error occurs.

I know that I am incorrectly pulling information from the store to update a member variable, because I am feeding it slices from the object store instead of copying the data. I'll change this later. However, even if that is an incorrect usage, the store object should definitely be returning the correct information whenever I access it, not keying into the wrong item in the dict, as I am seeing it do. The second time I key in, with the same key, it produces the correct information. This is incorrect behavior for any kind of dictionary. Always on the first try it is wrong; always on the second attempt it is correct.

The same error occurs whether I use get or wait.

### Source code
```
# To reproduce an instance of the bug:

import numpy as np
import psutil
import ray
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scipy.spatial as sp
from tqdm import tqdm

try: ray.init()
except: pass

class Distances:

    def __init__(self):

        # A 2D, mock embedding space with 32 points:
        self.space = np.array([                                                         
            [ 0.00, 4.00 ], [ 0.25, 4.00 ], [ 2.00, 4.00 ], [ 2.50, 4.00 ],
            [-1.50, 3.00 ], [ 2.00, 3.00 ], [ 4.00, 3.00 ], [-3.00, 2.50 ],
            [-2.00, 2.50 ], [-1.75, 2.50 ], [-1.00, 2.50 ], [-2.00, 2.00 ],
            [ 0.00, 2.00 ], [ 3.50, 2.00 ], [-1.00, 1.00 ], [ 1.75, 0.50 ],
            [-3.00, 0.00 ], [ 1.00, 0.00 ], [ 3.00, 0.00 ], [ 1.00,-0.50 ],
            [ 2.00,-0.50 ], [-3.00,-1.00 ], [-1.00,-1.00 ], [ 1.25,-1.00 ],
            [-1.00,-1.50 ], [-1.50,-2.00 ], [ 1.50,-2.00 ], [ 3.00,-2.00 ],
            [-0.50,-3.00 ], [ 0.00,-3.00 ], [-2.00,-4.00 ], [ 4.00,-4.00 ] ])

        # k neighbors to calculate: furthest, nearest, and second-nearest:
        self.make_kth_neighbors = [31, 1, 2]
        self.metric_str = "euclidean"
        self.neighbors = None


    # Convenient graphing function so you can see the data and who should be
    #   nearest, second nearest, and furthest neighbors to whom:
    def graph(self):
        plt.figure(1, figsize=(5, 5))
        p = plt.axes([.1, .1, .8, .8])
        p.scatter(self.space[:,0], self.space[:,1])

        p.set_xlim((-4.2,4.2))
        p.set_ylim((-4.2,4.2))
        p.set_title('Fixed Cluster-like 2D Test Set')
        p.grid(True)
        p.set_aspect('equal', 'datalim')

        for i, tup in enumerate(self.space):
            p.annotate(str(i), tup)

        plt.show()


    def kth_neighbors(self, k):
        if k < 0: k += len(self.space)
        assert k in self.make_kth_neighbors

        # NEIGHBOR CALCULATIONS IF HAVEN'T BEFORE:
        if self.neighbors is None:

            self.neighbors = {} # dicts keyed to the k we're told to calculate

            for k in self.make_kth_neighbors:
                self.neighbors[k] = np.empty(len(self.space), dtype=np.uint64)

            # PARALLELIZATION TO FILL IN NEIGHBORS:

            # Calculate the required neighbors for the object with the given index i:
            @ray.remote
            def neighbor_row(i, space, len_space, metric_str, make_kth):

                distances = sp.distance.cdist(
                    np.atleast_2d(space[i]),
                    space,
                    metric_str
                ).squeeze()
                ordering = np.argpartition(distances, make_kth)

                nbrs = np.empty(len(make_kth))

                for j, k in enumerate(make_kth):
                    nbrs[j] = ordering[k]

                return nbrs

            # Shove some data into the object store:
            space_id = ray.put(self.space)
            metric_str_id = ray.put(self.metric_str)
            make_kth_id = ray.put(np.array(self.make_kth_neighbors))

            # Start the remotes:
            remotes = [neighbor_row.remote(
                i,
                space_id,
                len(self.space),
                metric_str_id,
                make_kth_id
            ) for i in range(len(self.space))]

            # Get the results:
            for j, vals in enumerate(zip(*ray.get(remotes))):
                self.neighbors[self.make_kth_neighbors[j]] = np.array(vals, dtype=np.uint64)

        # Return the kth-nearest neighbor of every object in the space.
        #    Since self.neighbors is a dict keyed to k values,
        #    the same k should ALWAYS produce the same array!
        return self.neighbors[k]

d = Distances()
print(d.kth_neighbors(1)) # NOTE: without print, this one doesn't print,
print(d.kth_neighbors(1)) #    while this one does. Not sure why, but it may be related.
```
Output:
```
[ 4  2  5  5  8  3  3 11 11  4  9  9 14  5 12 20 11 15 15 23 19 25 25 20
 25 22 27 20 25 24 25 26]
[ 1  0  3  2  9  2 13  8  9  8  4  8 10  6 11 17 21 19 20 17 23 16 24 19
 22 24 23 26 29 28 28 27]
```
You should know that the first array it gives are actually the values that kth_neighbors(2) should produce; ie, the second-nearest neighbors. This appears to be what it gives even if I put in 31 as well.

issue_title: [jenkins builds] please limit the number of CPUs you reserve during your tests
issue_body:
### System information
- Linux Ubuntu 16.04)

### Describe the problem
when your tests run on jenkins, it looks like you calculate how many available cpus are on the machine, and then allocate some to your builds.  this can lead to over-subscribing CPUs, causing massive system load.

![ray-load](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1606572/43800247-b1283d02-9a44-11e8-806c-167b3fa1a82b.png)

your builds are pushing system loads to nearly 200...  and w/only 48 HT cores on these boxes, there are ~150 threads waiting for available CPU cycles.

since we currently allow more than one Ray build per node (PRB and master), this can really lead to CPU bottlenecking and increase system latency.

i would strongly recommend putting a cap on the number of allocated CPUs to a much more reasonable number...   maybe 6 or 8 at the most.

### Source code / logs
from https://amplab.cs.berkeley.edu/jenkins/job/Ray/4277/consoleFull : 

== Status ==
Using FIFO scheduling algorithm.
Resources requested: 17/48 CPUs, 0/0 GPUs
Result logdir: /root/ray_results/default
RUNNING trials:
 - A3C_PongDeterministic-v0_0:	RUNNING

issue_title: Possible memory leak in Ipython when using %matplotlib inline
issue_body:
I am using Jupyter notebooks to access the the ADGCv2 or Opendatencube, as it now is called. 
I did some band math, masking and so on with S2A-L1C data and plotted the results using matplotlib. Suddenly I got a ``MemoryError``.  When I checked my CentOS machine, all 32GB of memory where permanently allocated. When I restarted the kernel in the notebook, all the memory was freed again.

I investigated this a bit and it seems there is a quite serious memory leak happening when using ``%matplotlib inline`` in a Jupyter notebook. Every time I plot a figure with matplotlib, some amount of memory is allocated (e.g. 2GB for a more complex plot) but not freed after the plot is finished. When plotting again the some bahaviour is shown, leading in a bigger notebook to a ``MemoryError at`` some point. 

I could find a unresolved ticket regarding this problem in the [Ipython repo](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/7270)

In the above ticket, it is suggest to use ``matplotlib.use('agg')`` instead of ``%matplotlib inline``. This indeed solves the memory leak, but I always need to save the figures to the disk in order to see them. It would be way nicer to see them directly in the notebook. 

I am aware that I can restart the kernel when the ``MemoryError`` occurs and then go on with my calculations, but this is not a very convenient solution either as I would need to load all the needed products again. 

Has anyone else experienced the some problem and/or found a solution?

issue_title: Add a query of spatial and/or temporal bounds for a product
issue_body: For some grid based workflows, it is useful to know the bounding box for a query, to then work out what cells are inside that grid. There is currently no function that can use the database index for each dimension to work out the bounds. Getting the time range is also useful for previewing what data is available e.g. for the dashboard.

issue_title: Load should fail earlier when requesting "too large region"
issue_body:
### Expected behaviour

When requesting to load region larger than available memory `dc.load` should fail quickly before attempting to read any data.

### Actual behaviour

`dc.load` spends considerable amount of time loading some of the data, then eventually fails due to out of memory errors. If say you requested to load bands `red,green,blue,nir`, but you only have enough memory to hold `red,green,blue`, `dc.load` will allocate and load data for the first 3 bands (one at a time), but then will fail while trying to allocate memory for the last one. It can take minutes to find out your request is too large. 

### Proposed Solution

- Allocate destination array ahead of time for all the bands of interest, and only then start loading data into pre-allocated storage. Ideally allow user to supply pre-allocated output as well.

- Provide convenience methods for estimating memory needs for a given query result

issue_title: Deadlock detection
issue_body:
Our `Lock` objects enforce the constraint that they can only be released by the same task that acquired them. We might (probably should) add a version of `Semaphore` that enforces the same constraint (see discussion in #156). We should take advantage of these constraints to provide better error detection. In particular:

# Detecting never-released locks

This is pretty trivial, yet useful: if a Task exits while holding a `Lock`, then this is a bug (that `Lock` can never be released), and we should alert the user. (I guess technically it's not a bug if this is the only Task that knows about the Lock, but I don't think requiring people to issue a spurious release in this corner case is too onerous if it helps catch errors in general.) This would be pretty straightforward – just keep a record of the lock-or-lock-like objects held by each Task, and then when a task exits, if this set is non-empty, whine loudly.

Not entirely sure if this means "print a warning" or "raise an exception". There's also the option of forcibly releasing the lock, though I'm dubious about the wisdom of carrying on once your distributed system has lost internal consistency... maybe poison the lock so anyone who waits on it gets an exception, and if people are following good Erlang style then they'll all get rebooted and all may be well?

# Full-fledged deadlock detection

We can also detect classic deadlocks by tracing the wait-for graph.

This scope would be limited, e.g. we can't detect deadlocks caused by two Tasks that are using two Queues for communication and are both blocked, because we don't have any way to know that *that* task there that's blocked is the only one that can send on this Queue. Maybe we should! (E.g., by having the concept of binding a Queue to a Task so only that Task can send on it.) But even if all we can detect are mutex-based deadlocks that's still potentially very useful!

There are a lot of possible variants on this. We don't want to add too much overhead; I don't know if tracing the wait-for graph on every contended acquire is fast enough to be acceptable. ([It worked for these people](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/13759#issuecomment-168326434), but that's a pretty different context.) It's probably a reasonable thing to try first, though. (One can also do stuff like trigger a trace only after a task has been blocked for N seconds. Though in practice keeping track of this on every contended acquire might be just as expensive as tracing the graph immediately, if the graph trace usually finishes almost immediately.)

Another thing that can be useful to detect is we see one Task acquire A-then-B, while another Task acquires B-then-A. This by itself doesn't *prove* that there's a deadlock, but it's certainly suspicious, and can help catch latent bugs before they hit.

Again the basic data needed for all of these is the ability to look at a Task and get a list of all the lock and lock-like objects its waiting on.

issue_title: Gazetteer example has a poor recall rate (under 28%)
issue_body:
I get a poor recall rate when running a Gazetteer example which I've created based on pgsql_big_dedupe_example.
The code for reproducing the problem is attached. Please see the "instructions.txt" file for instructions.

     https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/files/1134932/Gazetteer.Problem.zip 

The code actually uses the results of pgsql_big_dedupe_example. For every cluster returned by Dedupe.MatchBlocks (having more than one record), one donor is selected for the "gazetteer" while the other donors are used as "messy data". It means that for each "messy" donor, there should be a match in the gazetteer which was already  identified by Dedupe as the same donor (when pgsql_big_dedupe_example was run).
Under these circumstances, I would expect this Gazetteer example to have a very high recall (and also precision). But while the precision is indeed high, the recall is very poor.

The "instructions.txt" file also contains instructions for checking the results to calculate the recall ratio, 
 which is under 28%.

I use Dedupe version 1.7.0, with Python 3.5.3.

The recall argument for Gazetteer.train is 0.9 .
The threshold for Gazetteer.matchBlocks is 0.5 .

Notes:

	1. Because of issue #578 (which wasn't fixed yet in 1.7.0 - see issue #587), the blocks are created 
              using StaticDedupe.blocker rather than Gazetteer.blocker. 

	2. The file gazetteer_problem_settings is included in the set of files. But if deleted, the example code
	    would re-create it.

	3. The file dedupe_example_settings is also included, for creating the StaticDedupe instace.
	   If deleted, the example code would NOT run. However, it may be re-created by running 
             pgsql_big_dedupe_example using the same training JSON file: gazeteer_problem_training.json .

issue_title: Add a release procedure file
issue_body:
We should probably add a `release-procedure.md` file to this repository

An example

*  Ensure supported Python versions in `setup.py` and `.travis.yml` are corrects 

*  Ensure windrose version is up to date in `version.py`

*   Tag commit and push to github

using Github website

Go to https://github.com/python-windrose/windrose/releases/new
tag: vx.x.x

or using cli

        git tag -a x.x.x -m 'Version x.x.x'
        git push windrose master --tags

* Verify on Zenodo

Go to https://zenodo.org/account/settings/github/repository/python-windrose/windrose

to ensure that new release have a DOI

*  Upload to PyPI

Ensure a `~/.pypirc` exists
```
[distutils] # this tells distutils what package indexes you can push to
index-servers = pypi
    pypi # the live PyPI
    pypitest # test PyPI

[pypi]
repository:http://pypi.python.org/pypi
username:scls
password:**********
```

Upload

        git clean -xfd
        python setup.py register sdist bdist_wheel --universal
        python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel upload

* Verify on PyPI

Go to https://pypi.python.org/pypi/windrose/
or https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=pkg_edit&name=windrose

issue_title: next actions cannot distinguish between parent model and the original model
issue_body:
if actions are connected to 2 models, `initial` and `followup` where `followup` has ` FK to `initial`, the `followup` model eventually cannot be created because the `FollowupAction` does not distinguish between the parent (either initial or followup) and the original action (initial). that is, a followup can spawn a new followup so in the case:

initial -> followup(1) -> followup(2) ->followup(3)

followup(1) creates OK since the parent (initial) is the same as the FK
followup(2) fails since the parent (followup) is not the FK (initial)

Need to separate the concepts of parent, which may be `initial` or `followup`, and the FK, which is always `initial`

issue_title: feature request: Async DDPG
issue_body:
I'd love to see an asynchronous version of DDPG on here. Would anyone be able to help me with it?

Here are quick thoughts:

A3C seems to be king of the hill at the moment, but DDPG has some clear advantages in terms of generalizing better, and ultimately is very similar in its implementation to A2C.  It just doesn't multithread, so A3C can win with greater musclepower. But if DDPG had a readily available and easy to use async version as well in Keras-RL, I think it would get a lot of widespread love.

However, the A3C implementation in the experimental branch never quite got done, so it's going to take some effort to get that up and running, then rework it into an Async DDPG. I think I know the problem though.
In the [paper,](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.01783.pdf) Deepmind states that all the actor / critic layers should be shared (i.e. with common weights) except the last one, but in this A3C implementation, the actors and critics seem to have independent networks. I suspect that's the reason there are issue threads here regarding its performance. The shared weights seem to be a fundamental mechanism by which these asynchronous threads work together toward the common goal, so with the actors and critics being independent networks, it seems like memory recall with a common pool of memories would be the only thing binding them together. I could be wrong though. 

Next, one of the main differences between A3C and an async DDPG is the fact that DDPG takes action as an additional input with state in the critic network.  However, I'm not entirely sure if you also need to share the core layers of the actor and critic networks in an async DDPG implementation the way you're supposed to with A3C, and if you are, how you would do that. I saw one implementation (which I'll have to find again) where the output of the critic was simply multiplied by the action input right before the last layer, but that doesn't totally make sense to me. In that implementation though the actors and critics did share weights up till that point. I do see how maybe you could have two neural networks (one built just on state, and a simpler one on action with maybe just one layer) that merge together, and then have maybe 1-2 additional hidden layers before the output. I'm not sure if that would totally work, if there's a better way to do it, or if that's just totally not necessary.

I did find another A3C implementation built with Keras that has worked well for me. [Check this out.](https://github.com/rlcode/reinforcement-learning/blob/master/2-cartpole/5-a3c/cartpole_a3c.py). You can see that the actor / critic layers are shared, though they did diverge for more than just the last layer. In my version, I kept it like in the paper and all layers were shared except the output (critic just has the Q value as output, and actor gets a softmax vector). For async DDPG, I think it would be a similar network setup, just with a more complex critic network that also works the action in somewhere. I just don't know how many layers should be shared with the actor(s).

issue_title: RDFa Parser Update
issue_body:
lindstr...@gmail.com, 2009-08-06T23:09:24.000Z 
The RDFa Parser has been replaced by integrated pyRdfa code (we have permission to do so by Ivan 
Herman). The previous parser was quite old and didn't conform to the recommendation. (I also got 
an ok by Elias Torres who made the initial implementation.)

I have completed an initial integration of this and committed it to trunk (rev 1691). These are the 
changes in short:

 * Removed "rdflib/syntax/parsers/RDFaParser.py".
 * Added new new "rdflib/syntax/parsers/rdfa/" *package* (new RDFaParser in "__init__.py").
 * Adapted "rdflib/plugin.py" to use new parser.

The code is mostly verbatim copied from pyRdfa, with some minor cleanup and documentation 
adaptation. This may need further work (doc format, code conventions), but that might be better to 
do in a coordinated effort (to encompass more parts in need of cleanup)?

Tests have been adapted:

 * Removed old "test/test_rdfa.py" and "test/{ntriples,rdfdiff}.py" (only used by old test).
 * Emptied current "test/rdfa" and added a new test module along with a subdir for a copy of the 
W3C RDFa testsuite.

Furthermore: the class "IsomorphicTestableGraph" has been moved from 
"test_sparql/BisonSPARQLParser/test.py" to a new module "rdflib.graphutils", and renamed to 
"IsomorphicGraph". Does anyone have a better name and/or location for that?

(.. Note: IsomorphicGraph is currently used to make up for sparql bugs which cause some tests to 
fail where they should not. As of this change, all rdfa tests pass.)

(.. Also note that the "BisonSPARQLParser/test.py" didn't work before and still doesn't.)

I would like to have this change reviewed.
